reviewText:
There are few first Person games that encompass role playing and and shooting as well as this title does.  Yes this one offers the same sorts of things that games like the latest Fall Out games have.  But in this game you portray the part of what is called a Vault Hunter.  You go to a far off world to hunt down rumors of this Vault which is believed to be a cache of possible alien technology, or treasure or something even better.  A company int he game supposedly had found one once before and used the technology to make itself into one of the biggest companies around.  It has to be understood the game takes place inthe far future, meaning the ability to clone or even create items out of pure energy is entirely possible.  But it isn't an exact science and so its seams only a few people have that technology and are very stingy with it.  Anywise, the game runs very well onthe xbox 360, the resolutiona nd graphics are excellent, however maybe due to how a PC can show much more visual detail on the 360 version there are points when you look real far away and the game blurs the images or fades them....i don't personaly think they needed to do that...but they did anywise....its anot a big issue.  The controller for the 360 works almost exactly the same way a controller for the PC version works....but better....as in onthe 360 you can move the map with the thumb stick...cant do that on the PC.  Game play is first person shooter-esque.  With some places in the game having hidden boxes and such that might require a little more Mario-esque jumping skills.  Its alot of action...there is no turn based any thing in this one.  This i also would say is veryu adult in nature...with some of the htings the enmies or Non-player characters say very adult oriented.  But also there is some very interesting humor.  This game is very easy to play.  And if you have never played it before or anything similar you should be able to start playing immediatly and have fun.  There are alot of extras put into this game as well...like for example there are not only the typical xbox achievements but there are in game achievements that reward alot of experience points.
Ah forgot to mention the game is a level based game....you might if you read the instructions get baffled...from one thing....and thats the skill points.....they don't start giving them to you until you reach a level of 5...fairly easy to do....but i admit i was alarmed that i wasn't able to use my class skill until that point.  I have played all the characters....i prefered the Berserker the most becasue wlel i loved how he punches....heals and does tons of damage and can take alot of damage....my second favorite was the ex-soldier....becasue i found after a while he couldn't run out of ammunition...siren was third....as she is fast but well....alot of people like to play her...so maybe just my choice...hunter was last choice....go figure...i just liked playing a alrge screaming punching tank more....so the others didn't get played as much.

I could go on and on onthis game....its has alot of replay value to it...and downloadable content....hughest level inthe base game is 41...downloadable content raises that to 63...

So I highly recommend this game.
summary: One of the best games to play

reviewText:
I wasn't sure if I'd like this game or not but thought I'd take a chance.  When I first started playing, I became more skeptical and I didn't really enjoy it a lot, but I kept with it and soon it grew on me and started to become more fun.

The graphics are very nice, buildings and streets are as good as I've seen and comparable to GTA 4 which came out in 2008.  NYC is very nicely represented and you can recognize many of the buildings and settings.  The people and car quality for graphics wasn't quite as good, I would say more on the level of GTA San Andreas which I think came out in 2005, so a bit of an older look for those aspects.

Running around on free mode is fun for a while, jumping over buildings, consuming people and taking on their bodies, etc.  After a while it does get a bit old so its time to move on to the main mission and the many many side missions.  You complete the side missions to get exp points that you use to upgrade your skills.

I liked the progression of the disease from seeing a normal peaceful city in the beginning to a city filled with disease and panic near the end.  Billboards are normal at first, but by the end are all about the disease, its a nice touch.  I liked the way the story was presented too.  It starts at the end so like any good movie, you jump in on big action, then you go back to day 1 and build up from there.  The way the story is presented is intriquing.  The main character has lost his memory and as he learns what happened, so do you!  I had kind of figured out where it was going early on but I do that with movies too and it didn't ruin it for me.

That's the good.  Now a couple of minor issues.
1. The controls for PC are just way way way too complicated.  There's tons and tons of different moves, combos, devastators, etc and there's all kinds of combo keys to hit to make them work.  I couldn't remember them all.  It's like if you double tap a directional key and hit the left mouse button and then E this happens but if you hit the right mouse button and F something else happens.  When you're in the middle of a battle, its seriously hard to remember all that.  This having been said, I was still able to complete the main mission on normal mode and most of the side missions even with my more limited arsenal that resulted from not remembering what buttons to hit for what move.  Seriously though, this was just way too many options to keep them all straight and sometimes I just hit everything and hope for the best.

2. This is a personal issue but I prefer games that let you customize your character.  I realize GTA doesn't do that and I still enjoyed those games as I did Prototype, but I really was able to identify more with my character in Saints Row 2 for example, because I created her look and stuff.  I have trouble to identify playing as a male character.  I can do it but its more fun for me to be a tough butt kicking girl!

3. Despite the open world of Prototype, I still felt it could be kind of linear in a way and when it came to the side missions, they tended to be rather repetative.  Sure, there were a few different kinds of side missions.  Glide toward a target, fight as infected, fight as ally, consume events, etc, but within each type the variation was minimal so you were kind of doing the same things many times over.  I also thought I'd see the kind of free for all battle royale that was hinted at in the beginning of the game, near the end but that never really materialized.

4. There's a little bit of music in the menus but otherwise there really isn't any.  The sound effects are terrific, I love them but some music would have been nice too.  Music did a lot to help define games like GTA, Saints Row and Dragon Age for me.

Anyway, I was pleasantly surprised ultimately and it did turn out to be an enjoyable game.  If you like GTA or Saints Row type games, you may enjoy this one too.
summary: It Grew On Me and I was Pleasantly Surprised

Much better deal, especially for the lifespan of the DS games.  (ie: kids finish them or grow out of them or constantly changing interests).  This is one of the rare instances I am not loving amazon's retailers.
summary: My 7 year old LOVES this series - BUT.  .  .

reviewText: I've been playing Civ since Civ I.  This is clearly a much evolved game since that time, but the fundamentals are still the same--explore, build, conquer, invent, invest, dominate, destroy, negotiate, win.  It is the most complex, most delightlfully devilish of games.  You will happily throw hours down the drain and wonder where they went while in the throes of this game.  It is wonderful.  Graphics are superb, the play interface is good, and the challenge ranges the gamut from insanely easy to demonically hard.  Civ 4 was great.  Civ 5 is better, and I think it is the best of the Civs since Civ 2.  If you like games like this, this is a no-brainer.  Get it.  It is amazing.
summary: Most beautiful iteration of CIV, more fun than ever

reviewText:
I really did enjoy this game. I wish it was longer, though. I cleared the main story, doing and redoing web of challenges to try and get more gold medals. Out of the 68 challenges, I have a gold medal on 48 of them. Doing this, going for some achievements (42 of 47 as of now), and beating the whole story, I am at 81% completion and I would say have put in 12-13 hours of gameplay. The core story shouldn't take more than 8 hours, though. It was a ton of fun but the web of challenges are sometimes unfair and frustrating. I would recommend this game as an instant purchase to big spiderman fans, but a rental or a purchase at 30 or less for anybody else just looking for a good game to pass the time.

8/10

EDIT:
Okay, so I have gotten every achievement and finished everything. Totaled up to about 20 hours. Was good fun, but hopefully Beenox can deliver more with their next game coming on July 3, 2012. Open world here we come!

I like the game the Multiplayer is now getting better with the party chat, but there are some things that I don't like. For example the bullet spray, and the story was kind of sucky. The game graphics are good and the 3D.

INTRO
I'm really kind of sad to have to write a negative review for this game.  Herinteractive, the company behind the production of this game, had finally started to make some really good adventure games after creating several duds.  Unfortunately, they appear to be going back to Dudsville as they produced an inadequate game with this 25th offering in the Nancy Drew adventure series.

STORY
-The main idea of the story is really good.  You, as Nancy Drew, have been framed for an explosion and fire at the River Heights Town Hall.  Based mostly your lack of alibi and your matches found at the scene of the crime, you are tossed in jail on grounds of arson.  It's up to you (now as not only Nancy but also Bess, George, and Ned) to prove your innocence and find the true culprit.

-Okay, so the framing of Nancy is quite genius; however the execution is terrible.  I mean, you're thrown in prison because of matches and no alibi?  Even after you prove other people don't have alibis and they have circumstantial evidence you still have to stay in jail?  Although you're suspected of this huge fire, you're allowed to roam the entire prison as a free person?  I mean, did Her not think these things out?  It makes no sense.  Yes, it is a fictional game, but give me SOME realism.

GAMEPLAY
-Like all the other games in the Nancy Drew adventure game series, this is a first-person point-and-click adventure game.  The main difference in this game is that you are able to switch between multiple characters.  The way to do this is extremely choppy, though; you can only switch through Nancy.  For instance, say that you are Bess but want to switch to Ned; you can't just call Ned and switch to him.  That would be too easy.  You have to first call Nancy, talk to her, switch to her, then from her perspective call Ned, talk to him, and finally switch to him.  It took way longer than it needed to and was really annoying.  The good news is that you won't be switching characters much.  The bad news is that the reason you don't switch characters much is because there is not much of a difference between any of the characters!  Why have all these multiple playable characters if it really doesn't make a difference to the gameplay?!  It's pointless.  Why couldn't Her have made it a bigger deal?

-The suspects were another thing Her butchered.  While 2 of the 4 people you investigate are really unique and interesting, the other two character are poorly designed ripoffs from the books.  Deirdre Shannon, from the current Nancy Drew: Girl Detective series, is portrayed much more helpful, friendlier, and smarter than she actually is in the books.  Brenda Carlton, from the older Nancy Drew Casefiles, was completely changed personality-wise.  There is nothing to be recognized about her at all.  When Her used to use characters from the books, they at least stayed true to them...why can't they do that now?

-Okay, but how does the investigatory gameplay work?  Is it fun?  Not really.  While I can't really say that all the objectives in this game are chores, they really aren't puzzles either.  There are very few logic puzzles thrown into the gameplay, and they're get old quickly.  The meat of the gameplay is just going from character to character, giving them the third degree.  There might have been more sleuthing if Her had added more locations to the game.  Unfortunately, there were only about 5-6 small places to investigate.

-I wouldn't necessarily call this game short; it comes in at about 5-6 hours, longer than some past Nancy Drew games.  However, this is one of the rare games that I wasn't fully invested in and didn't really care about replaying when it was over.

GRAPHICS/SOUND
-I'm not even going to say much about the music other than it was boring, soft, and unexciting at best.

-The graphics were wonderful; they have truly proven to be Her's strongest feature.  Everything is vibrant and pretty to look at; plus, it doesn't always look all that fake.

-The voice acting was brilliant on some characters, lacking on others.  It was definitely better than some games overall, but I'm still waiting for the game that has flawless voice acting from all.

OVERALL
If you've made it through this entire review, it's pretty obvious I was disappointed and discouraged by this game.  There was not much remotely mysterious, exciting, or adventurous about anything in this game.  Without giving anything away, the ending isn't even that daring.  I rarely say this, but I can't recommend this game to anyone but those extremely dedicated to the series.  It's not the worst of the 25 games Her has created, but it's down there in bottom 3.
summary: Not worthy of being the 25th Nancy Drew game

reviewText:
Naughty Dog basically got Jak and Daxter: The Precursor Legacy and smooshed it together with Grand Theft Auto between two pieces of bread, creating a hefty and delicious videogaming sandwich which is sure to provide gamers with a nutritionally good gaming experience. Food references aside, this game blends the qualities of the The Precursor Legacy and GTA; namely the platforming and combat of the former, and the free-roaming and vehicle stealing of the latter. So while Jak II does change things considerably from The Precursor Legacy in terms of overall presentation and gameplay, it still manages to remain faithful to its predecessor. At the end of The Precursor Legacy, Jak, Daxter, Samos the Sage, and his daughter Keira opened a Precursor door atop Gol and Maia's Citadel and stood in awe of what lay within. This game opens with the group back at Samos's Hut with what they found in the door set up outside the hut: a Rift Rider. When they activate it, a portal opens, out of which a swarm of strange flying creatures comes out, along with a large beast who appears to be after one of our heroes. The Rift Rider then takes the group into the portal, with Jak and Daxter getting separated from the other two and landing in a large, industrial metropolis known as Haven City. Is this the future, another planet, or another dimension? Play the game and you'll find out. Anyway, Jak is immediately arrested by the Krimzon Guard, a Storm Trooper-like security force who serve the tyrannical Baron Praxis. Imprisoned for two years, Jak is pumped full of Dark Eco by Praxis, who hopes to create a "dark warrior" to combat the Metal Heads, dark mutants who seek to enter Haven City and slaughter its inhabitants.

After being rescued by Daxter, the duo escape into the city and join an Underground resistance whose goal is to dethrone Praxis and free the city, although Jak's goal to stop the baron is driven more by vengeance than the city's well being. By the way, Jak finally speaks in this game. When Jak and Daxter's journey begins, they'll go on missions for the Underground and other interesting characters to sabotage the baron's schemes and defend the city from the Metal Heads. The missions you undertake in this game all vary greatly: you'll shoot down flying enemies and ships inside a gunpod, take part in high speed races and chases, hunt Metal Heads, escort allies to safe zones while outrunning the Krimzon Guard, escape exploding buildings...the list goes on. Most of the missions are also very challenging, and a handful of them are incredibly difficult. The most difficult mission in the game by far has Jak running through a pier with many intersecting walkways and an endless army of Krimzon Guards coming at him from every direction, giving Jak no alternative but to shoot his way out. Despite the game's difficulty, I always found myself itching to undertake the next challenging mission, since they're all a blast to play. The game's enemies and handful of challenging boss fights add further to the game's fun factor. The Krimzon Guard who patrol the city function like cops and will attack you if you attack them or the citizens; even if you accidentally crash into one of them, the city will go on alert and they'll chase and shoot at you. When this happens, you'll have to either outrun the Krimzon Guard or stay out of sight for a few moments until they call off the search. Outside the city, the Metal Heads are your main enemies, and there are many kinds of those creatures: four legged grunts, scorpion things, hornet-like bugs, cloaked hunters, and other freaks of nature; and they're all fun to kill.

Jak himself controls the same as he did in the first game, with his jumping, climbing, and fighting abilities all intact. But he's got some new tricks up his sleeve as well. As mentioned earlier, Jak gets to shoot enemies in this game. After the first few missions, Jak gets a morph gun with four different firing modes utilizing Red, Yellow, Blue, and Dark Eco as the ammunition for each mode. He'll start with the Scattergun (shotgun), next the Blaster (rifle), then Vulcan Fury (gatling gun), and finally the Peace Maker (brings instant death to non-boss enemies). Each weapon is useful against certain enemies and specific situations. Also, thanks to Praxis's Dark Eco experiments, Jak can absorb concentrated amounts of Dark Eco from fallen enemies (falling into pools of Dark Eco still brings instant death) and temporarily transform into Dark Jak, where his skin and eyes turn dark and he grows claws and a pair of horns. In this state, Jak attacks faster and harder, and he can utilize Dark Eco to destroy large groups of enemies in one fell swoop with shockwave and electrocution attacks. Traveling in this game is sort of its own adventure. First off, Haven City is HUGE and has lots of winding streets and paths, so you'll have to utilize a handy in-game map and follow icons to your destinations if you're to have any hope of not getting lost.

To get around, Jak and Daxter can ride zoomers that are similar to the one in the first game, only in this game there are many different kinds of zoomers, some large and some small. They can either hover just above the ground or ten feet in the air. Jak can..."Jak" zoomers from people or take unused parked ones. Later, Jak gets a jetboard which is slower than a zoomer, but much more maneuverable and is actually needed to traverse certain areas and complete missions. It functions like a skateboard and allows you to do Tony Hawk-like tricks and jumps; you can even hover over pools of Dark Eco with it. Navigating yourself through the city streets can be irritating at first, since they're normally crowded with lots of other people riding zoomers, and the lousy drivers make no attempt to get out of your way if you're moving at high speeds, which several missions require you to do. In fact, it's hard NOT to crash into other zoomers in this game, but it helps build the tension in certain timed missions to know you have to be very careful if you don't want to fail the mission. The game looks great too, whether you're traveling through Haven City's bazaars, ports, slums, sewers, and parks, or if you're outside the city exploring forests, mines, and pumping stations. The character designs are similar to the ones in the first game, only smoother and more detailed. Jak II even retains the first game's cartoony sense of humor, once again utilizing Daxter as its main source of comic relief, while adding somewhat more mature content and language, but nothing offensive or inappropriate. So yeah, this game has an interesting story, beautiful graphics, very challenging and action packed gameplay, and humor. Really, what more could you ask for in a videogame?

I applied the protector to the bottom screen without much trouble. This protector works perfectly! I can't even tell that it's there, and the touch sensitivity is just as great as before!

The top screen, on the other hand, was really difficult to apply. Since it covers the entire top half of the 3DS, it's just too big to be able to put on without getting some kind of dust or bubbles under it. I eventually gave up and decided to just use the bottom protector. That's the more important one anyway, since that's the touch screen.

In reply to some of the other reviews on here, these protectors arrived in perfect condition, with no scratches or anything. All stickers and labels were exactly as they should be.

If the top protector was just big enough to cover the top screen, instead of the entire top half, I would have rated this 5 out of 5.
summary: Bottom Screen: Perfect! Top Screen: Too difficult to apply

reviewText: This one is slightly different in play mode. The one big thing is if you are shot you are a goner...no second chances and it plays more like a sniper game in other words you really really have to sneak up on folks and make sure you aren't targeted...if you're lucky you might be able to find a medic and heal yourself but don't count on it...so in fairness this game takes a bit more forethought..and it seems a little baren in other words there is not a lot of people running around like lets say Call of Duty or Medal of Honor...and in default mode it feels like you are by yourself out there...it takes a little getting use to...and speaking of different Company of Heroes plays like a chess game..that one really takes getting use to....

reviewText:
Before I go into the review, let me give you a little background so you can consider my experience and bias when it comes to this MMO. I loved EQ 1, the game that exposed me to MMOs in the first place and opened my eyes to the possibilities of an online world with others. I have also enjoyed City of Heroes, Lord of the Rings Online and Uncharted Waters Online. When it comes to Bioware, I really enjoyed Dragon Age Origins (NOT DA2), Mass Effect 2 and Jade Empire. Of those three, I enjoyed Dragon Age Origins the most. Finally, when it comes to Star Wars, loved 2 of the 2 original movies, enjoyed the first 1.... Find the last 3 worth a late night nothing else on watch.

Now that this is over, let me start by saying this is not an Sandbox game. If you are looking for a hit the road and explore as EQ offered and Uncharted Waters Online also kind of offers (UWO is mostly a strategy title though), this is probably not the game for you. If you enjoyed Mass Effect 2's style, minus the combat which is very different, and would enjoy playing in the Star Wars Universe, might be worth having a look.

There are two sides to choose form, the Jedi and Sith, basically good and bad although, as you play the varying class stories, each class, 4 for Jedi and 4 for Sith, have their own stories, you will see that some of the choices and events happening make you stop and wonder, if only for a moment, what is really "good" or "bad". The good is the classes do play differently although their mirror counterparts are very much the same. I play a Smuggler which is basically a long range DPS. I also played the Imperial Agent on the Sith side in beta which plays very similar to the smuggler. Although there were early claims from Bioware about maybe not relying on the holy trinity, healer, tank and dps, they still do BUT the trinity is not required to complete the game. You can finish your class quest, enjoy a lot of encounters with other people and due to companions or some hybrid classes are fine without the trinity. If you are taking on flashpoints that are higher than you, like my group does, or just hitting areas that are purposefully challenging, the holy trinity is more important but still not 100% required like others. I play a DPS. I do fine on my own, my companion (all classes are pretty much "pet" classes") tanks for me and if I join up with others, and I am still very functional. I also have a very handy mez against droids so versatile on some levels too.

The two sides have interesting stories, but the Jedi, at least at this time, seems more flesh out to me. They play differently because of the story, but beyond that... not that different.

Character Creation

Lacking. There is a variety, yes but not really on par with other game releases. I play a cyborg. While I could get a single eye replacement on the Sith side, I couldn't with the Jedi. I know where I wanted my implants but there was nothing I could do to get what I wanted. Hair choices are limited, skin tone is a good choice. In a world of aliens... surprising lack of choices, EA/Bioware defended this choice earlier on... I still don't buy it. It is certainly possible to run into your exact twin... unlikely. There are games out there that such a choice would be one in maybe a million, but these are not this game. I've actually found myself chasing after the wrong group not realizing it. Everyone looks too similar. The clothing choices, the color scheme... even heights are all so drab it's unfortunate. Don't get me wrong. It is a nice looking world but it's just lacking in this department.

Flashpoints

Flashpoints are my favorite, by far. They are basically instances where a group, usually a full group, spends about an hour trying to complete some sort of mission together with ample reward and sometimes death. They are challenging and fun, and the death penalty isn't so great to try and avoid them. You can repeat the missions, and we do, challenging ourselves to do better each time until we move onto the next one. The only real down side to flashpoints is Jedi or Sith... they are, at least as far as I have gone, the same. Sure the story is different, but the map is repeated, the bosses are even the same and it's not really something you could play on the "other" side and feel you are getting a fresh experience.

Crafting

I am a crafter. I craft in every game and enjoy it. Having said that, crafting in this game is so easy and non-intrusive even those against crafting are enjoying it. You don't need to spend an hour each day crafting or, as some would see it, not I, not playing to get crafting down. Your companions can do it. If you are in a full group not using your companion or have more than one, you can send them on their way making things for you, getting things for you and you can pick up supplies/material as you are playing without delaying your whole group for long.

Inventory System

Sucks, it really does. I feel like I have fallen back into some sort of early 2000s game that has no clean way to organize my stuff. It's just a bunch of squares that don't sort nicely. You have to turn on, in preferences, your ability compare your companions gear to the gear you have acquired and only then if they are with you. I haven't found a way to compare my ship upgrades to what is acquired at all and have to manually toggle between that.  The nice step up from the early, early days of MMOs is the mission items are separated into their own tab, thank god, and the currency you can use to exchange for items is also separated out, though slightly buggy. I have one commendation I can't spend or destroy or do anything with. Customer Support has yet to personally respond to me on anything I have inquired about. I don't know that they will ever address any of my minor issues.

Spaceship Combat

I was pretty against the rail, watered down system that was released with this game. I still find it... lacking on a number of levels. For one, it is a single-player only. When I play these mini missions I just keep wishing others on the massive online game could play with me. The controls are simplistic and the challenge is... there but not in a strategic way so much as itchy trigger finger way. There is some fun to be had. Because they have this minimalistic space combat in the game... I fear there may never be a richer offering.

Bugs/ Servers

Some servers seemed to have 2 hour waits. Mine had 20-30 minutes tops on peak days. I just come home, clean up, eat, whatever while I que and have plenty of time to play after work or during the weekend. I have not found the queues to be a problem at all.

Bugs, yes there are bugs. One of our flashpoints, we couldn't tell if my long range attacks was being read as an exploit or bug which made a particular boss incredibly difficult at times or standard at other. There are bugs in either case. The mail system is shockingly slow. The eyes of characters keep disappearing in my conversations. The leaves are flashing like mad around me.  We had a treasure chest not unlock during the flashpoint. Having said that, this has to be one of the cleanest MMO releases in recent memory. This might even be one of the cleaner just plain game releases for me in a while. The industry is just so used to release buggy, shoddy, barely working products to fix later after they've taken our money that I wondered if they cared at all about what they are putting out on day 1 anymore. There is no reason to wait for this game to be "cleaned up." It's pretty good already, although I suspect Slicing (crafting skill) will be nerfed at some point. I should also mention I thought Rift beta'd pretty cleanly as a reference and Star Trek Online at release was a disaster.

Solo vs Grouping

Although I question why some people would join an MMO to play solo, it's entirely possible. You can play 1 or 2 or 3 or 4 and enjoy all thoroughly. You will want 4 for the flashpoints, but you won't be held back by them. I've played in mixed level groups, had plenty of fun too.  While the xp and loot is garbage, I've played grey quests (no xp) to finish off story lines. The Flashpoints are the reason why these side missions and my class missions turned green or greyed out on me.

Overall, this is a nice MMO to play, for veterans and certainly new entries into the genre a like. Is it everything I hoped it would be? No, not necessarily. For all the story emphasis, unlike the single-player RPGs, our choices in the storyline have no real effect. It's an MMO after all, and we're not at the point where player choices are changing the world in any meaningful way. It's hard for me to real get drawn into a story I feel I have no real... "choice" in that matters, but not impossible. I am not sure what EA/Bioware is going to offer to justify their monthly pittance, possibly nothing. This might be another pub claiming they need 15 dollars a month to house data and offer servers... I hope that is not the case. I know what servers cost... and it isn't 15 per person.

This game has a lot going for it out of the box. That says a lot for any PC game and certainly bodes well for an MMO. I hope they don't drop the ball and figure their job is done and it's time to nickel and dime players to death, read milk to death, and offer no new content and overpriced expansions. It is my hope that Bioware, and EA will let them, will treat this game and their player-base, well. For now, I am willing to ride along and ride with that hope, enjoy the game while doing so, and hope to see the rest of you online.
summary: What a Great Release to a Fun, Hopefully Long-lived Game

reviewText:
I grabbed this during a pre-order special and don't regret my purchase.

There are plenty of other full reviews on here so I will keep mine simple.

I have very few cons for the game, but the ones I have are as follows.

reviewText:
I bought this game a while ago and played the single player for a few months before even trying out the multiplayer...Big Mistake.

This game is one of the most fun and interesting multiplayer 3rd person games since Gears in my opinion. Players get a series of different 'back packs' that alter the players mobility, strength, speed, etc. My favorite has to be the Rhino Pack which allows you to run through buildings. I haven't checked out all the modes but they have their own versions of most online game modes these days. Its a nice break from the standard shooters out there.

The destruction engine in this game that allows you to tear down virtually whatever you want is  beyond welcome. You could run around for hours just smashing things into oblivion.

CONS
*Single player can get repetitive and possibly could have been a bit more developed/planned out.
*Cars/Trucks don't drive all too well
*Slightly less people play on the multiplayer now.
*Landscape is cool but not as 'terraformed' as others may like it to be. Environment could be better.

PROS
*Tons of weapons to cycle through (4 at a time in the X,Y,A and B slots).
*Multiplayer is addictive and amazing.
*This game is unlike any other game out there.
*Gameplay is fast paced, fun
*Lots of missions in Single Player.
*Destruction engine is fantastic! Destroy everything in your way!

All and all its worthy of being in your collection. You probably won't play it every chance you get but once and a while you'll crave walking around on mars able to tear down full buildings and a multiplayer that makes you think "Why can't I put on a rhino pack and just run through an entire building in the real world?". This game rocks!
this games rocks my face off. and so did the price i paid for it too. came in perfect condition, and i beat the game in a week.... again.

Ah, yes...the Sega Genesis. While Sega did have stupid add-ons for it, as well as graphics and sound quality that isn't as on par as the Super Nintendo (although that system was made a couple years later), there was a colorful selection of games out there.

Even if a few were terrible, the majority was awesome, if not just good in general. As years have gone by, Sega kept re-releasing a handful of these games, which are found in collections like Sonic Mega Collection and the PSP's Sega Genesis Collection. But, I must say, the finest of them all is THIS piece of wonder.

This collection is Heaven for a Sega gamer. Right away, without unlocking anything, it has 40 games! Are they bad? Well, there are unwanted games, like Alex Kidd and ESWAT, but DANG!! FORTY Genesis games??? How can you not like this offer? Even the Phantasy Star games and the Sonic games are present! There's also Kid Chameleon, which is a good Mario clone, and the Streets of Rage trilogy. I could just keep yapping about each game shown on the list, but I haven't even gotten to the unlockable ones yet!

If you think 40 games is enough, think again: There are 9 unlockable games, resulting in...OH MY GOD!!! 49 GAMES! Granted, one of them is an arcade version of Altered Beast, despite the Genesis version already being available, but still! There's Space Harrier, the original Phantasy Star, Golden Axe Warrior, Congo Bongo (answer to Donkey Kong), and Shinobi!

So, simply put: Buy this collection!! How can you say "No" to almost 50 games in one disc???!?!?!
summary: Do you REALLY want to pass by this?

reviewText:
Honestly, this is the first game on PS3 that I could actually spend 60+ hours playing, and STILL want to keep it up!
I decided it was high time I wrote a review about this game, because it just doesn't make sense to me how people can say this is a bad game, or worst game in the series - don't get it. While I understand where they are coming from, they also have to understand that not all games can be the same. Of course, when a new series-based game comes out, you're looking for it to be the same - yet better than the previous one. It can't be that way all the time.

Personally, I love this game, I can't say it's my favorite Final Fantasy, because it truly isn't.
But in no way will I say this game is terrible and disgraces the Final Fantasy name - because it doesn't.
I don't compare games to each other, because really, it's not fair at all. To me, it feels like you're not appreciating the game itself, rather just saying it's better or worse than it's predecessor. I look at what the game, personally, has to offer. Not what it didn't have that others did.

But anyways, I'll start with the story.

*STORY*
The story in whole is interesting, yet boring at times that come quite too often. But I'm not the type of gamer that will put a game down because it's boring, I give the game time to recoup or regain my interest, which it did. Even though it is boring some times, it does still remain it's interesting plot. The story is told in a nice way, although confusing sometimes, I guess until you get to the end. I like how the game has movie scenes every so often, because I like a good story, and not many games have such in depth story lines anymore, so I personally liked it.

I never felt like I was actually IN the game, but it was amusing watching the cut scenes, and I really liked the characters relationship with one another, and how they talked to each other, and all that. It made me feel happy, for some reason, that they all were getting along or having fun - or something. But to me, the story is pretty much the same as always, but it is told in an interesting way, and I liked it very much - especially the ending.

*CHARACTERS*
Ok, so the characters themselves are pretty nice. They all have nice personalities and such. It's 6 playable characters, Lightning, Snow, Sazh, Vanille, Hope, and Fang. And a non-playable character, that the story pretty much revolves around, Serah. My favorite character is Sazh, and then probably Hope and Vanille. Now I read that Lightning's character was modeled after Cloud Strife, from FFVII, I didn't know that at first, but it really wasn't done well at all. What I mean by this is, even though Cloud was the silent type, he still had a lot of dialogue in the game (or at least I THINK he did) even if it was '...'. But Lightning on the other hand, it's not necessarily her character, but it's the way they implement her into the game; She's supposed to be the main character, but has little less than 20% of the dialogue in the whole game.

She talks, of course, when spoken to; but they hardly speak to her within the whole game. While I don't personally have a problem with it, it is a bit weird seeing as the character Fang (who comes in way too far in the story) makes for a better main character than Lightning, and she comes into the game halfway through! But like I said, I don't personally care, I just find it strange.
People say that the character Vanille is way too cheery and annoying, while I could agree, I don't find her THAT cheery or annoying.
To me, she's just a Selphie or a Rikku; she has her ups and her downs, and her ups are usually fronts - you'll understand all soon enough. But personally I never found fault with her, or any of the characters really.

*Gameplay*
This is basically what has this game thrown overboard. But wait, it's not because the gameplay IS bad, it's because reviewers and critics have MADE it bad. Saying that it has super linear gameplay, as if it's some monstrous, demonic, SESAME STREET CHARACTER.
I can say, that the game doesn't have big open areas that branch out into different areas at every which way, but so?
If you look at other games such as FFX, it's no different at all. There was only one way down Mih'hen Highroad, and only one way up Mt. Gagazet. What's the problem? Well, there isn't one really. The only things that this game doesn't have, (though I'm not comparing),
are towns in which you can go to shop or take quests or whatever.

You can shop, of course, but I'll get to that later. So there is no Balamb, no Luca, it's just field, basically.
While there are towns in the game, they aren't the same as other games, they put you into towns but there's really only 1 way to go, and there aren't any merchants or no shopping district or anything, it's strictly storyline when they put you into a town.
But I don't have a problem with it, because it's just the way the game is made. (Even though it's not like I don't have problems with ANY game because it's just the way it's made.)

People say the game 'Opens up' at Chapter 11, and in the end-game area, what this means is - without ruining the story - that at chapter 11, you go from Cocoon to Gran Pulse (Cocoon is a world floating in the sky, visible from Gran Pulse as like a moon, and Gran Pulse is the giant, wild-life-filled outer world. And so at chapter 11, you go to Gran Pulse, and the areas there are open, even though it's not THAT many different places you can go to, for me, it's still enough. And at the end of the game (Chapter 13) there are portals in which you can revisit 2 places: Gran Pulse and another place within Cocoon. Even though the game only pretty much has Story and Hunt quests, it's not like the game is empty, I still have fun farming for money/CP (exp equivalent), probably because this is the first console game where I actually had to farm! But anyways, I still find it fun hunting all the monsters, I think it's about 64 or 63 marks in total, but when you start getting to around 40-50, it's when you really start having to farm and get a little stronger. So it's cool.

*Battle*
In a way, Battle has been changed in this game. I say in a way because its's still ATB, like FFX-2, but it's different.
Now how it is, is you play only with the party leader, and the other 2 characters are played by the AI. Don't worry, they are played pretty well, even though they can only use Abilities, not Techniques or Items, so you end up having to use potions to help them after so long. But anyways, your character has an 'ATB Gauge', you start the game off with 2 bars. 1 skill uses about 1 bar, but more advanced skills may use 2, or 3. At times in the story, you gain an ATB gauge, the max you get from completing the story is 5; but their ultimate weapon gives you 1 more, so a total of 6. But it's costly/hard to get an ultimate weapon, so you'll have 5 for a good majority of the time.

The battle pretty much becomes battle when you have to switch Paradigms. Paradigms are like templates of Roles, a Role is like Medic, or Commando, or Sentinel, or whatever. There are 6 roles in total. Anyways, a Paradigm is when you choose what roles the 3 characters in your party will use. Example: A Paradigm that consists of Commando, Ravager, Ravager can be switched to in battle. You can set up to a total of 6 Paradigms, but can always change them/delete them in the main menu (when out of battle).

The battle isn't won by attacking or using a spell though, a key to winning is to fill an enemies Chain Gauge, and stagger it.
A Chain Gauge is this: Enemies have a bar, 'Chain Gauge', and every time you attack the enemy, the gauge fills. Ravager's build the chain gauge fast, as it's a passive ability they have. But the chain gauge will decrease as fast as it increased if only a Ravager is attacking. Commando's can slower the time it decreases by attacking the same enemy as the ravager. Saboteur's can do the same as a Commando and Ravager, but it's a lot less potent. They can't build a chain gauge alone, but they can slower it a little bit - a lot less than Commandos, but still, a little bit. And when you get the gauge filled, the enemy becomes Staggered. When their staggered, they can basically be destroyed, their defenses are lowered, and most of the time, they become susceptible most status ailments.

Overall, it makes the battle much easier. But this really makes for a fun, challenging battle, and ALWAYS. You never really get a feeling that you're overpowered against other monsters, because really, if given a chance, they would scrape you - and hard. But if you go about it right, everything will be fine.

*CHRYSTARIUM*
A Chrystarium is the name of the thing where you develop skills. Some say it's like a Sphere grid, I guess it's like that, but just a bit worse. Anyways, you unlock the ability to actually change roles at Chapter 3 of the story, and it did make the game much more fun.
Each character starts off with their main role, and throughout the story they unlock about 2 more. You can only choose from all the roles halfway throughout the game, meaning that Sazh only has 3 roles until the middle of the game, in which all 6 roles are unlocked.
It's ok I guess, even though I kind of wanted them to follow different role paths, but it's fine. What I really dislike is that not all characters can learn the same skills. Each character basically has a main Role, so if you max Sazh as a Ravager, he has a few of all elemental skills, but his main ones are like Firaga and some other aga, and if you maxed Snow as a ravager, he'd have Blizzaga and some other aga as his main ravager skills. This is really annoying because you can't develop a super ownage character, and it basically forces you to use certain characters in certain battles, because they all have something the other one doesn't.

Especially when you have your favorite character, and you can't even use them that much because they really aren't much of an asset to the team. Even though it's ok for the most part, I still would have liked to have that 'I AM ALL POWERFUL' feeling.
The Chrystarium has 10 levels, which are all unlocked throughout the story. Every time the Chrystarium levels up, you can learn more skills. You unlock level 10 after you beat the game. (When you beat the game, it asks you to save, then when you reload that save, you'll be before the boss battle in terms of story, but after the boss battle in terms of points gained and stuff. Then you just run and go through a portal to start the farming and side-quests.

*MISCELLANEOUS*
The game features Eidolons (Aeons/GF's); each character has 1, and they can only be used by the party leader. So the AI won't use them, only the person who's Party Leader is able to use it (because it's a Technique, and AI can't use Techniques).

Sadly, even though their summons, they aren't strong at all. You'd be lucky if you can kill the easiest mobs with the Eidolon's "limit break". Really. Very lucky. Really, though, they make a good asset to battle because, when summoned, your 2 AI partners disappear, and it's just you and the Eidolon in battle. The reason they make a good asset is because 1) they use Curaga when your HP is low, so the only times you can really die is when your taking major, quick damage. 2) when you die, they use Arise, which makes the Eidolon disappear, but revives you. 3) they have a mode called Gestalt mode, in which you basically can't get hurt because for some reason the enemies can't even target you, but anyways, your Eidolon turns into whatever kind of vehicle, you get on, then you have a selection of different skills you can use. For example, if you hold the left analog down, then press X, you'll do this move. Or if you hold the analog stick left or right, then press X, it'll do a different skill. And if you press Triangle, it does their ultimate move. Even though it is in no way an ultimate move - in terms of damage, it still looks nice.

And most importantly of all, when your summon is de-summoned in any manner, you, and your party will be completely healed.
One bad thing about summoning them is that if the enemy is staggered or has a chain gauge near full, using the Eidolon's ultimate attack will clear it, and what's left of the stagger on enemies. So you'll have to restart filling that gauge, which is bad if you're fighting a hard monster. But it's fine, I GUESS.  I'm just sure we all would have liked to see Eidolon's do 100x more damage.

You can upgrade your weapons, and thankfully, all weapons can be upgraded to an ultimate weapon. How it works is, there are 3 tiers of weapons. 1, 2 and 3, the third is the ultimate weapon. It doesn't matter what weapon you start with, but each Ultimate weapon is different, depending on what weapon you start out with. If you start out with a weapon that greatly reduces max HP, but has stronger offensive power, and upgrade it to an ultimate weapon, then that weapon will do a lot of damage, but in turn, it will reduce your max HP by 70%. If you start out with a weapon that allows for faster chain building, then the ultimate weapon will come out with faster chain building. Even though you'd probably only be able to afford 1 catalyst needed to transform a Tier 2 weapon to a Tier 3, there still are various types of ultimate weapons, even though they retain the same look and name no matter what weapon you start with, the effects all differ.

Gil. Gil is hard to come by unless you farm it. If you beat the game without doing THAT many side quests, then you could easily have about a total of 500,000 gil left. Maybe less, depending on whether or not you buy materials to upgrade weapons. The only way to get gil on here is to farm certain enemies. Which you can easily do once you get to the portal section of the end game area. You can farm one enemy, which is tougher than a fat baby (no offense) or just go a simple route, getting a nice amount of money each run (about 80k), and it doesn't last that long. But anyways, it's no deal breaker, it makes it worth farming though, which is a nice difference in games.

You don't actually gain the ability to 'switch your party/party leader' until like halfway through the game, which is kind of annoying, because you might want to play with Sazh or Snow at some points, but they automatically force you to use so and so.
It's ok I guess, but even more so in the beginning of the game I wanted to play with certain people.

I easily spent 70 hours on this game, and plan to spend quite a few more hours. It's actually fun trying to see how I should go about this next battle, or what I should upgrade.

*OVERALL*
In total, I give this game a 4, because it just messed up on a few things, like not being able for all characters to have at least MOST of the same skills, etc. Even though it can easily be given a 5, I just can't. As far as people saying 'YOU'VE BROUGHT DISGRACE TO THIS FAMILY'S NAME' goes, they can shut it all up. Never through the game did I say, wow, this game ruined Final Fantasy for me.
Whoever says that never was really connected to the series anyway, just wants to say something. Of course we all have our personal opinions, and you can hate the game deeply if you want, but to say that 'I'm never playing Final Fantasy again' just because one game out of the series is actually different, then what does that say about you? Even if this game was bad, and all the innovating stuff wasn't innovating to me, I still wouldn't say 'DISGRACE'. I'd just say, man they really messed up, I'll have to see what's next.

We all are different types of game players, I'm the type that gives a game time to develop, and appreciates it for what it is, and not downing it because it's different from it's counterparts. I actually like story lines, I don't always like jumping in and slashing and shooting everything in site. Personally, I play a Final Fantasy game FOR the story line, I don't understand how anyone can expect this game to be something it's not. Of course it doesn't have the same aspects as others, but it still does a wonderful job at what it has. And I had, and am still having a lot of fun with it, and am glad I spent money on it.

If you don't like 'BORING STORY, LINEAR RAWR!11!!1!', which this is non of anyways, but you just need something to blame your dislikes for the game on, then you probably shouldn't get this game.
But if you're like me, and don't play that many RPG's and was looking for something to slip on, then get this. It's very fun, interesting, amusing, and most of all, it's one of the few games that remains fun after beating it. Good luck, hope this helped.

reviewText:
Long ago an elegant cruise ship named the Queen Margaret was hit by a tsunami type wave which deluged the ship and buried all aboard in a huge wall of surging sea. The stricken ship became a prison for shipwrecked souls who could not escape. The ship, without a live person on board and submerged at the bottom of the sea, is attached forever to a tropical island nearby.

As a reporter you visit the Santa Rossa island when weird rumors of ghosts and deaths abound and you are to investigate the phenomena. A good friend, Jane, who would be your guide, went ahead of you, but she too has disappeared.

The artwork in this intriguing and beautiful game is top notch, the story is great, but prepare for the most diabolically hidden objects ever. The items to be found appear whole at the bottom of your screen, but they are divided into teeny weeny pieces and you're going to need eyes like a hawk to find them. The hint mechanism, however is very forgiving!

You are going to be kept very busy trying to solve the mysteries, gathering items to help you and utilizing them in the right manner. You learn that a wicked shaman was responsible for the huge wave that engulfed the ship and is waiting for his chance to send another one to destroy the island. Ghosts from the ship materialize as you go, warning you that the shaman will strike at the eclipse of the moon and the countdown begins, you have to hurry, hurry! You save each ghost by finding an artifact particular to him such as an artist's palette or a violin for a musician, a spyglass for a sailor. When the ghost is "saved" he becomes a little wooden doll which you place in the proper room of a model ship. We are reminded of Alawar's "Stray Souls: A Dollhouse Story".

As you tramp around as fast as you can, checking out a wide variety of scenes, all beautifully rendered, you will discover journals, letters, posters, notes scattered over the entire island and reading them adds depth to the game. You have your hands full here! As usual in a HOG game you are a world class mechanic and handyman. The mini-games are not hard but they're entertaining enough. Throughout the game you'll be carrying on in a way that could be called frenetic. You've got to stop the shaman and his black magic and find your lost buddy. The game is immensely complex and detailed and it's a wonder the producers were able to coordinate it, but they did! Don't miss this one!

Im trading this in , its not worth keeping another 5 min . This is a complete waste of money , time and energy . I would be disappointed if I had rented this , to bad for me I bought new .

The Nintendo Wii, it never lived up to it's potential. I was so excited for this system at it's launch. I waited in line with my cousin over night at Target to be one of the first to buy it. I picked it up with Zelda. Even from day one I was unimpressed... with full hype-goggles on I knew something wasn't so hot about this system.

I booted it up, with my official Nintendo brand the competent cables(that cost extra) so I could play in 480P... I knew it wasn't going to look as good as the 360, but I didn't care.  I just cared about the games. Much to my surprise when I hooked everything up just to come to the realization of how cheap it all felt. The sensor bar(which I didn't even know it needed at first), the look and feel of the menu's & hardware. The Wii-mote with this tacky wired dongle attached to it.  It all felt like some cheap Chinese knock-off system, but it wasn't, this was the new Nintendo hardware!  I couldn't believe it.

Then I played Zelda for about 8 hours straight the next day. The whole time I wonder when it was gonna start getting good, at the time Zelda was by far and away my favorite franchise so I knew it couldn't be this dull. I kept playing, then I bought some Virtual Console games, then a few other "AAA" titles eventually came out and I bought them all... never quite feeling satisfied with them. They all felt very uninspired, and were missing that magic Nintendo of old had in their games.

Then I started realizing that the whole system experience was like this for me; dull, uninspired, cheap, a side-step instead of a leap forward. So I sold it, and with the exception of Mario Kart, I haven't missed it at all. I watched as my idea of what I would want from Nintendo was constantly beaten with the truth of what Nintendo had become. They sold Wii Fits, and Wii Motion Plus', and Wii plastic shotguns, and put out Wii condoms, and added more warning screens, but they never seemed to stop all the non-sense and put the focus back into creating a new classic Nintendo master piece.

I've been more than happy with my PS3/DSi set-up. I get my serious gaming on my PS3, and my Nintendo fix on the DS. I also have a 360, but I'm becoming increasingly more displeased with that system after having 2 break on me, and now all this Kinect junk being pushed, but that's for another review..

What's left for the Wii... Skyward Sword? A game which everything I've seen from it looks like a poor excuse for what the Zelda franchise used to be. It's look like a full on waggle-fest, puzzle game. Not the great action adventure with light RPG & puzzle elements of old.

Nintendo use to design controllers for games to make them work better(Super Mario 64), now they design games around their gimmicky controllers to sell more copies to the curious casuals(Wii Fit).

There's little value in the first-party game library, and hardly any value in the third-party library. There's primitive motion controls (seriously, Wii MotionPlus in an attempt to make them less primitive?). The download service has gone from okay to piss-poor. And this was supposed to be the future of gaming...

You have to access the Steam server to access the disk that you have in your computer.  Since the Steam server is not very reliable I will not buy this product.

Update:  I have given Steam a another chance.  If buying a product that uses Steam, buy it as a download through the Steam service.  It seems to work a lot better than buying a retail version.

Been playing this for two days now, and I am very pleased so far. [Update: I've been playing for a week now, and I like it more and more as the days have gone by!]  People have spoken a lot about how things are different than Dragon Age: Origins.  I'm going to assume that you know that, and I'm going to focus on what I have enjoyed about this game so far.  First--the graphics are fantastic.  We just finished Final Fantasy XIII, which had spectacular graphics, and then we started Two Worlds II.  The graphics on Two Worlds II were such a comedown that we had to stop the game for a while so that we would stop expecting FFXIII style graphics.  Well, it looks like Two Worlds II will have to wait a little longer, because upon popping in this game, I was blown away by how beautiful everything looks. Movements are smooth and natural, the scenery is crisp and deeply detailed.  Monsters are terrifying.  Facial expressions are better than DA:O.  Sometimes moving in and out of the cut scenes is not as smooth as I would like, but that is a problem that no game designer has solved to my satisfaction yet.

Next, the music.  The MUSIC!  I am a fan of film scores, and a fanatic of classical music.  This is probably one of the most interesting scores to a video game that I have yet heard--pieces of it are like parts of a concerto, with a solo instrument rising from the swell of the orchestra.  I looked into the composer, a man named Inon Zur.  He has composed music for a large number of other video games, and I hadn't realized how much of his music I had heard before--Prince of Persia, Dragon Age: Origins, Fallout 3--and I will now make a point of listening to his scores.  Really enjoyable, even apart from the game.

Voice acting is top notch.  I miss Claudia Black, from DA:0, of course, but aside from that (you can't have her in everything!) the cast is spectacular.  Actors include Miranda Raison, from the BBC's excellent series "MI-5", Nicholas Boulton, who plays the male version of Hawke here, and who played various voices in DA:0. He was also on the excellent BBC Series "Doc Martin".  Joanna Roth, also here from the BBC's "MI-5", and Rebecca Stanton from the BBC's "Tess of the D'Urbervilles" and "Jane Eyre" and a host of other good actors play the voices.  These are all working actors, none of whom have struck it rich but all of whom know their craft well.  They are given a good script and have taken it above the camp or kitsch it could have been and made it engrossing.

The dialog wheel has received some comment--people have said that it is "dumbed down".  I don't think that adding icons has made it any less intelligent, but it does give the player a little more understanding of what is going to be said and can help make the best choices for the path you wish the game to go.  I am actually happy with the icons next to the vocal parts, and have no issue with it.

Combat is fun.  The combat runs smoothly and is exciting, but not too complicated.  That suits me perfectly, as I am not great at remembering fancy combinations--but I do want to look cool as I kick the Ogre's rear end.  Spells are very colorful and exciting, sound effects are great.

Storyline has really engaged me so far, but I am only two days into it.  I love getting lost in a world and lost in a story, but we also love side quests--there appears to be plenty of both so far.

If you enjoyed DA:O, you will likely enjoy this game as well.  You should not expect it to be the same as before--there are differences, and some you may not like as much--but coming to this game with an open mind is sure to leave you impressed and pleased.  I look forward to many more hours of fun my first play through, and I'm already anticipating what I want to change for my second (or may be third!) play through the game!  From a guy who does not play most RPG games more than once, that is about as good an endorsement as I can give.

----Update:
After having this game for about a week, I've really enjoyed it even more than I did on my initial review.  Navigation from place to place is a snap, and I appreciate that you don't have to spend a ton of time navigating from game area to game area.  I appreciate the way that quests are easily outlined and you can follow them in any order you want from place to place.  This makes things a little less "realistic" than Oblivion was, for instance, but makes playing a little more streamlined, and this i a compromise that I think is for the good.  Combat is really enjoyable, and the tactics are quite a lot of fun.  I love getting more tactic slots almost as much as I enjoy getting other leveling up treats.  The voice acting continues to be a standout for me, and the characters are a lot of fun.  I love the Dark Emporium (don't manhandle the urchin, he's not for sale...get your own!), and I enjoy the cut scene animations--beautiful and well done.  I wish there were a little more control over the weapons/armor for the secondary characters, and I don't really understand why they took this away, because I play them almost as much as I play Hawke.  Even so, this is not a deal breaker when it comes to the fun.  The story line has gotten a couple of dings from some reviewers for not being strong, but I'm not sure that it is any weaker than the previous storyline--I've been enjoying it a lot and I feel quite connected to the characters. It is especially fun when there are references to the events in DA:O.  Although you don't need to have played the first game to enjoy this one by any means, it does add a little spice, a little depth to the understanding of some of the political undercurrents in this adventure.  Ultimately, this game is as good as I had hoped, and better than I feared.  I can unhesitatingly say that it is a lot of great adventuring fun.

--Update:
My wife and I have now finished the game a couple of different ways.  I very much enjoyed the main story line, and felt attached to the characters as a whole.  I have a few criticisms after thinking carefully about the game, but my overall opinion is the same--it is quite fun.

My problems--Sometimes the "quests" or tasks seem a little random and not well integrated with the overall story line.  I don't mind a few random side quests that are completely stand alone, but I think that I would have preferred more side quest integration with secondary story lines.  This happened some, but could have been better.  After completing the game, I really did miss the chance to upgrade armor and weapons for my party members in a more controlled fashion.  Especially sad about Bianca--Bianca was cool and all, but she stayed the same while other available weapons would have been better, ultimately.  When you are going to give your crossbow a cool sexy name and then spend a lot of the game cooing over said crossbow, it had better be worth the inability to upgrade it.  We had a number of freezes.  Our PS3 is brand spanking new and so I can't blame anything but the game.  Freezes are unacceptable in my mind, and are a big turn off.  Ultimately, it did not stop us from finishing the game, but it is frustrating and takes you out of the moment.

I think that DA:II is a darn fine game, but it could have been better.  I do not rescind any stars, as it has held up already to repeated plays for us, but I hope that EA looks carefully at some of the suggestions (and anger!) from its fans and thinks carefully about how to improve the franchise.  Great graphics, great music, good story line, combat a little on the basic side, and limited upgrading options make this a cut above for the average gamer, but leave a little sadness for what might have been for those who are deeply dedicated to the franchise.  Play it, it's worth it, but let EA know how you feel about the changes so they can know how to focus for their fans.

This is the pinnacle of collectible Gears of War memorabilia, the Lancer! At 36" long, NECA was commissioned to create these Lancers to be exact replicas of their in-game counterpart. Using all the computer generated specifications and designs directly from EPIC, developers of the Gears of War franchise, this replica Lancer has been meticulously crafted to be a real world version of the uniquely designed weapon; it's a machine gun with a chainsaw! Series creator Cliff Blezinski remarked how they secured the patent for this weapon, and how if they were not able to secure that patent, there would be no Gears of War games as we know them.

When powered by three C-size batteries, this Lancer makes sounds that mimic those from in the game! A rare collector's piece, which was an Amazon.com Exclusive for the launch of Gears of War 2, the NECA Gears of War 2 Lancer replica is becoming harder and harder to find, and with Gears of War 3 recently released, it continues to appreciate in value.

reviewText:
When I first saw the screenshots of this Mario, I freaked out thinking "What the crap is Nintendo thinking? Mario with a hydro jet-pack?! Getouttahere!" But, since I'm a huge Nintendo addict and buy everything Mario (besides the Super Mario Bros. live action movie), I ended up reserving it for a cool Nintendo PC disc with a bunch of awesome Mario things and getting the game. I was really stoked the more I read about this game. When I actually started playing the game, I discovered it was a nice add-on to the Mario series... but I'd still prefer the classic jumping plumber over a weird water pack that helps you fly/rocket/etc.

The story is a bit different from the other Mario adventures. You are going on vacation and a graffiti punk begins to tag the resort with groovy colors. You're adventure takes you on a rid... a rid of washing paint of everything in sight! Now, I bet you're thinking "What in the..." but it's a fun, great Mario game. Still, I feel for you. The premier of a Gamecube Super Mario game should have been Mario in Mushroom Kingdom jumping on Goombas, using Yoshi to eat things, running from football turtles, and beating up on some Koopa kids. But, not here, although `someone' does make an appearance for you to beat the mess out of.

Graphically, this is a good looking game. Mario looks like he should in the 3D world with loads of polygons, loads of animations, and his classic red/blue apparel. Every other character you encounter looks really good too. The basic bosses are well done and that's where the classic Mario shows in the game. For instance, in one area, you fight a giant parana plant, who is really well done (really, really well done). The maps are really well thought out and look amazing. There's an instance where you have to battle on a roller coaster. The game never lags so everything on this ride of how the visuals showcase are in full proportion and really fast.

The game supports Dolby Digital surround sound. Mario + Surround = Awesomeness!! There's a few scenes where they actually did some good voice acting, staring Princess Peach, Toad, and Mario. I really have no complaint about the music at all. As for controls, this game is easy. The only time the game gets hard is the time they take the jet-pack off and you have to navigate some weird jumping puzzles. The reason it's hard is because you're used to the relaxing jet-pack and loose your ability to jump around. Intentional? I don't really know but those puzzles are really dang hard. Camera is the same. The camera is almost perfect in most areas (although some levels the camera is the thing that kills you).

There is some replay value to the game since you get to unlock an alternative costume, a more beach styled costume, to play through the second time. The game is well done so you may want to run through it again, just not right after you finish it.

Overall, I say get this game. It's probably $20 right now and well worth the money. I have never really disliked the game itself, just the fact that Nintendo hasn't released a classic Mario game for the Gamecube. The game though is well done and very fun. Also, in today's high-def age, the game still fairs well overall in the graphics/sounds category. Just don't expect the grass to move.
summary: Sunshine is a step down, but still fun

reviewText: I have a PS3 and a move controller and I own this game as well. This game is VERY problematic from the outset. When the game menu starts and you are useing the move controller to select the choices in the menu it is terrible. The marker does not want to hold steady for the choice you want to have. So it's like holding the shakiest gun in the west when using the move controller to select menu choices. Now to the golf game itself. Character choices are bland. The swing when driving and from the fairway work very well. But the problem lies with putting. For example when you are say 15ft away from the cup and the shot needs to go left or right a bit, moving the aim marker is a lost cause. You are suppose to hold the "T" button and move left or right accordingly. But just the slightest movement of your hand moves the aiming marker yards away from you cup rather than inches. This sooooo frustrating. The graphics of the courses are nice, but not great. More like the graphics of the PS2 days. All in all I would say this is a nice game but is problematic on controls.

Just Dance 3 for the Wii solely tracks a single controller in one hand while you dance along to a collection of songs. As simple as it might sound, it's actually quite a lot of fun!

I've played and enjoyed many dance games over the years. I still have a PS2 set up in the basement with a bunch of Dance Dance Revolution games, to play with those. We also have Kinect and a PS3 move. Each system has its benefits and flaws. The DDR games of course only care about foot stomping. Kinect can see your entire body, and doesn't need you to hold anything! This Wii game falls somewhere in the middle. You dance with your whole body, but the sensor is only in one hand, so it has no idea what the rest of you is doing.

On one hand I like how they have each dancer and scene customized to the song. A 50s-feel song (Crazy Little Thing Called Love) has a pair of kids dancing at a drive-in. Boogie Wonderland has a quartet of boogie-dressed dancers doing choreographed moves. You've got an undersea mermaid, "Respect" complete with 60s landscape, and much more. It really is quite fun, and the characters movements are incredibly smooth, too.

Each song even has dance moves appropriate to the song and era. You don't have random clapping / kicking / etc. that happens to be in time with the music. The moves really do "feel" like they would go with the song and era.

I'm also appreciative that the load times are quick. So many times when you're playing a dance game you have to wait 1-2 minutes between songs, which gets frustrating very quickly. Here it's nice and quick moving from song to song.

They have a great range of songs, so that young kids will find songs they like, and adults will have some as well.

So there's a lot of good. What's the flaws?

First, as mentioned, all the game senses is a single arm. They don't even have you holding a controller in each hand. So the game only knows what one arm is doing.

Next, I wish they at least gave you a few options amongst era-appropriate characters. Maybe different colors of skin and hairstyles.

Most importantly, though, the screen layout is not optimal. You have your character in the middle that you're supposed to match. You have a small shape in the bottom left which sometimes - but not always - tells you what to do next - you have to interpret its rather cryptic clues. Then you have an indicator in the top left which lets you know if you did an action well or poorly. It can be frustrating the first few times through a song because you have practically no idea what to do and you can't watch everything at once to know how you're doing.

So it's important to know that up front. Expect a learning curve for each song. You'll have to watch it a few times to figure it out and get the hang of it. Then once you can do the dance simply from memory, you can mostly watch the top left indicator to know if you did each move well enough to count.

I also wish they offered more feedback on WHY you didn't get a perfect score. Other games have a variety of ways to do that which is quite helpful. Here you're sort of on your own.

Still, the game is a ton of fun, a great way to stay healthy and boost your mood. It can be played alone or with friends. Well recommended!

I got this game today, and have played it for about 3 hours. I think it is going to be excellent. I have played throught the first "patrol" missions, which serve as tutorial material, and have started the first "traffic division" case. This game is NOT a "sandbox" game or a "shooter," or, really an "RPG." It is basically a simulation of being an LAPD detective, about 60 years ago.

The graphics are amazing, and the thing that is getting the most attention is the motion capture. I have never seen facial expressions like this before. This, combined with excellent voice acting (some of the faces are recognizable, by the way), make for a very unique experience. This isn't a game puntcuated by cut scenes. This is like watching a film. There are a few facial expressions that you will see, that are actually shocking, they are that real. The downtown part of Los Angeles is pretty well rendered (but it is like the 1945 version). I like that because I am from LA, and when responding to incidents, if I'm on Temple St, I know how to get to 7th and Alameda).

The people that have given it poor reviews, basically don't understand, or were not expecting this. They thought, I suppose it would be another "GTA." WELL IT ISN'T. It was PUBLISHED by Rockstar; but developed by another company. Yes, the controls are similar, but that's where the similarity ends. You start as a new patrolman, then get promoted to detective. Your captain assigns you and your partner cases. This involves responding to the crime scene, searching the scene for clues, including examining the victim, if the body is still there, examining any items that the first responders found and marked (with those yellow number cards), which could include shell casings, victims personal effects, items that could have been dropped by the perpetrator,etc, as well as looking all around for things the people first on scene may have misses. THIS TAKES TIME. Sometimes you will interview witnesses on the scene. THIS TAKES TIME. The combination of gathering physical clues, and interviewing subjects, lead you towards solving the case. You must try to determine if the subject is being truthful, lying, or leaving things out. If you challenge what they say, you must have already collected the evidence to back that up. You also sometimes must interrogate suspects at that station, using these same techniques. If you do everything correctly you solve the case, and move on to the next, and then eventuall, to another division, where there are new cases.

You and your partner, may encounter violent subjects, in which case you defend yourselves, but this is NOT a game based upon shootuts. They do happen, but it's more like being a detective in real life, most of your time is spent looking for clues, interviewing people, and putting it together. I am enjoying this a lot; this is a different experience from most games you play. As you gain skills and experience, I would think the cases get more difficult. Anyway, this is what happens in the game.

In closing, people that are expecting "GTA" or "RDR" this game IS NOT LIKE THAT. One thing I had to get used to, it is NOT a "sandbox" type game. You play through the story. You CANNOT go about doing random mayhem. (maybe that's why people don't like it). You cannot, for example, shoot someone on a whim, like you can in GTA. Your character does not draw his weapon unless he is fired on, or there is a risk to other people. You can NOT really be "evil" but hsi MAKES SENSE because your character is a decorated marine, trying to be an exemplary police officer. Shooting peds in the head because you feel like it, is not allowd. (You can still run them over, but that will get you in trouble.) One way that you CAN do other things, is as you are driving to the scene other radio calls will come over about incidents in progress; you are free to respond to these. I like doing these because I know the city, as I stated above. As we're heading down broadway, and I hear an inicident on San Julian, I will take it. These "random crimes" could be theives, gunmen, robberies, etc. Completing them adds to your stats.

This a a GREAT game. You just have to understand what is about. It's not GTA.
