reviewText:
A few days before getting this mouse I was commenting how gaming mice have gone from wired to wireless and back to wired again. And I wondered out loud  why no one produced a gaming mouse that can be both. I envisioned a mouse where a chord can be plugged in to use it wired or to charge the mouse or without as a wireless mouse.

The first thing I noticed on unpacking the Steelseries Sensei was that it has this very option '

It looks expensive and sophisticated with its metal charging station and fairly minimalist ambidextrous mouse design.  But compared to most of the competition there are not too many buttons so initially it may seem like it doesnt have many features.  However, as you quickly discover, that isn't the case.

Docking/Charging station

This steelseries mouse comes with an attractive charging station where you can place the mouse when not in use to let it charge. It is attractive and the beveled silver top is actually made of brushed steel that shouts quality. A glowing halo outlines the shape of the mouse and circles the inset space where the mouse sits snug for charging. The illumination is fully configurable, by default it is green and fades through orange green to orange through to red depending on the status of the battery in the mouse.

The USB cord has a braided cloth sleeve and  is a micro usb to regular USB type that can be plugged into either the docking bay or directly into the mouse itself to provide power.

It is a tiny connector but not as fragile as say a phone one. The plug and socket on the mouse and charger are slotted and the plug slides in and locks into place. So it won't wear or break easily despite the small size.

The Mouse

The mouse itself is an ambidextrous design that you cup in your palm rather than a claw grip. It has a rubber coated scroll wheel that is scroll only and does not have left to right movements (Not a big deal because in Windows, the default setting lets you press the scroll button to toggle panning of the screen both up down and left to right.) This does make the wheel very precise for scrolling and it feels solid when you press it as a button. The wheel has a very smooth friction with no play so it feels very good.

The main left and right mouse buttons are seamlessly hidden in the smooth body of the mouse and feel very precise with a muted click when pressed that sounds less intrusive than some.  Centered behind the scroll wheel is a single button for changing the CPI (sensitivity). You can set two different sensitivity settings and toggle between them at any time with this button. Eg., Toggle between fast movement for general looking around in a game and slow smooth movements for precision shooting or if you're doing Photoshop precise pixel manipulation.

On each side of the mouse you have two buttons. So a total of four extra buttons. This is really two fully usable buttons since the ambidextrous design means that unless you have a very dexterous pinkie finger you probably won't use the two buttons on one side often.

The mouse itself is quite light. It has an integrated battery which brings the weight down even compared to my last wireless mouse that only had a single AA battery.

The software/drivers for PC and Mac

The mouse does not come with a CD included so you have to download the Steelseries 3 engine software from their website.

Their software is very stylish and presented in a very elegant manner with intuitive interactive diagrams for controlling.

Sensitivity setting #1
Sensitivity setting #2
Battery life saving
Sleep timer
Sensor smart mode
Illumination smart mode
Acceleration/Deceleration
Lift distance
Angle Snapping
Polling Rate

There are various button profile programming options.

You also have full control of the illumination of the LEDs on the base and mouse logo through all its 16 million colors.

Individual preset/profiles can be stored separately and can be automatically backed up on to the free online cloud service steelseries provides their users. Great if you use multiple computers or game away from home like at lan parties.

In use.

The mouse is very comfortable cupped in your hand. The sensor is rated at 8200cpi which can be doubled through interpolation in the driver software.  The switches for the buttons are rated for 30 million clicks. They sound quieter with a duller more subdued click than other mice I have tried, a mellower sound which I think is nice and doesnt intrude so much if you don't like noise pollution.

You can use it as a wired or wireless mouse. I see no reason to use it wired when the polling rate is up to 1ms response time in both wireless and wired mode.

The mouse glides very smoothly over my cloth G series gaming mouse mat. It fits nicely in my hand and has a pleasant, slightly rubberized feel . It is very precise and responsive. With my ongoing carpel tunnel problems in my right arm, the ambidextrous design allows me to switch hands and use it comfortably in my left hand and rest the bad one. In games it also allows me to use it for looking around with my left hand while using a joystick in my right hand when controlling sim type games.

I have used a Microsoft and Genius gaming mouse that were left or right handed and this is easily the best.

Is it worth the price. If you are left handed, there is a limited choice of responsive lag free ambidextrous gaming mice and this is the best I have used. So if you can stretch your budget this is a good option.  Its also attractive and offers a fairly unique look that stands out from the crowd. It looks well designed and just looks and feels like a well thought out  quality product.

I highly recommend it.
summary: Looks and Feels Like a Quality Product

I spent $5000 building a custom gaming rig shortly before D3 came out, and the experience was poor because of THEIR servers.  $600 video card capable of running games double the resolution of this one, processor that could handle running a dozen virtual machines at a time and its all crippled at the kneecaps by their unjustifiable limits for no good reason.  $60 for a piss poor game, and ANOTHER $40 to almost make it playable - I think I'd rather just get my $60 back and spread the good word to my fellow gamers to steer clear of this stinker until they fix the largest flaw of all.

This game is what Shenmue 3 would be like if it had a bigger emphasis on fantasy. The dialogue and voice acting is better than Shenmue, but the things the character's so can be very strange and at times hilarious. (like when Carla goes up to someone and says "I don't know her" right in front of the girl's face. Another instance is funny where a guy asks if he can take Carla in his truck. It makes you ask: "WHAT???" Also the story is very interesting and exploration is a huge emphasis on this game, just like Shenmue. Dialogue does drag on for a long time. You often find yourself talking to one person for 5-10 minutes. It can be unnerving at times, but if you pay attention to the dialogue, it can make the world you're exploring more immersive. However, not as much can be said about the combat. It's very bare bones. Shenmue's combat is vastly superior to this, but Dreamfall's story is a lot more intriguing than Shenmue's story. (in my opinion). I liked Shenmue's story but this story was just fantastic. Talking to people was funny too. When my friend and I would play Shenmue, we would meet very weird people in the game and they have horrible voice actors and they said the stupidest things. That was one major charm Shenmue had on us. This game has it too, only the voice acting is better and the dialogue is longer and just as weird and pointless as Shenmue's (that's a good thing). It's weird but I would have preferred awful voice acting because that made Shenmue absolutely hilarious. Voice acting in this game is like voice acting in Doctor Who. It's all British, which is ok but it's not that amazingly bad voice acting Shenmue had. There was a prequel to this game that came out on PC about 5 years before this game and that was a straight point and click adventure game. Couldn't even compare that game to Shenmue, but it is very good for a point and click adventure game. If you pick up Dreamfall, be sure to pick up Shenmue 2, also on xbox. And the prequel to this game is simply called The Longest Journey.
summary: Fans keep asking for Shenmue 3, well HERE IT IS! You get the 3D graphical adventure game genre along wiht questionable dialogue.

I bought this to use for my PS2 with a 1080p HD TV.  Using the standard S-Video cable was unbearable, with grainy images and dull colors.

Upon plugging in the component AV cable, I immediately noticed a difference!  Playing Battlefront 1, I noticed details I hadn't noticed before.  The colors were more vivid and full.  It was like the PS2 was running HD games!

If you use an old standard TV, I'm not sure there would be a huge benefit using this cable over the S-Video cable, but if you are playing on an HD TV, I HIGHLY recommend that you get this cable.  It really makes a difference, and gives your PS2 games a makeover that they need (at least visually).

The cable seems to be constructed with high-quality materials, and should last a long time.  A plus is that it also works for the PS3, so you can switch between the two easily with one cable (although I recommend playing the PS3 with an HDMI cable).

If you have a PS2 and play on an HDTV, I highly suggest you order this if you are still using the S-Video cable!
summary: If you have a PS2 and HDTV, this is the cable you need

reviewText:
Time limits are how developers without any skill or talent try to make games more difficult or add tension if they can't think of any fun way to do so.

Crafting. I know some people like it. Ever played Minecraft? I haven't. I don't play games for the tedious boredom of mindless repetition. I don't know how bad this game is with it, I didn't get that far.

So, why I quit playing after ~3 hours.

<Minor SPOILERS>

The game begins with some background story and some tutorials. Extremely boring but you can hope once you finally get to play the game it might be fun.

There is a tutorial telling you how to equip items from a dispensary before leaving town. You seem to have two things you can equip, a grenade type thing and a healing item. I accidentally hit escape and the game forced me to leave without equipping anything.

OK, I figure I'll just go back before leaving town. No big deal.

When you go back, the game won't let you enter the dispensary anymore. I assume later in the game you can, but not now.

OK, I figure the items aren't that important and leave town.

Health does not replenish after combat. And it takes time to travel. And the whole game is a time-limit. So, after a few days travel my characters are mostly dead and I can't heal them. I can't use grenades in combat to help. So my only option is to travel back to town (losing days) or restart the game.

Really. I have to restart the game OR have the game artificially made extra difficult through the most-hated device of the non-skilled developer, time-limits, because I hit one wrong button when learning how to play the game?

I don't think so. So, I quit playing.

It is possible I will restart the game and try again this weekend but right now I don't think I will.

reviewText:
I'm not entirely thrilled with the software, which comes with a license key that gets permanently associated with your email address. It appears to let you install it on as many machines as you like, but this still pretty much kills any possibility of reselling the thing used. The device is capable of working with game cards from any region, but the software arbitrarily locks you into the region you set it to when you register it. Backup and restore appear to be done entirely locally, but the cheat code functionality requires an Internet connection and interacts with Datel's servers. Someone really needs to develop a 3rd party app for this thing.

The USB connection seems a little flaky, but aside from that it works well. I wanted a way to backup and restore 3DS save games so I can start a new save without losing the old one. This product delivers on that, and currently seems to be the only option if you want support for games that use NAND saving like Pokemon X & Y or Animal Crossing: New Leaf. And despite my gripes about the software, it's very easy to use.

When the USB flakes out, you get an error and not a corrupted backup, which I am very grateful for. I made multiple backups in a row and binary-compared them as a test. Apart from the header Datel adds, the data was identical. Saves are stored on your disk encrypted, but it's not something Datel decided to do. They just don't decrypt saves after dumping them from game cards.
summary: Excellent device, could use better software

Ubisoft has been one of the leaders in games with ancillary fitness benefits with its Just Dance series, but they've often fallen short when it comes up traditional exercise games. I remember in particular the abysmal Your Shape for the Wii; the Wii rendering of Jenny McCarthy still haunts me in my dreams.

So I admittedly wasn't expecting too much with Shape Up. But boy, was I pleasantly surprised.

Right away you can see that Shape Up makes good use of the advanced capabilities of the Xbox One and the Kinect 2.0. Granted they use a bizarre "8-bit" looking font throughout that gives the game a sort of cheapish "retro" look. But once you look behind the curtain there's some impressive technology.

You're greeted by two full-motion video instructors who'll be your teachers (and your competition) through the game. Speaking of full motion video, not only does the Kinect camera detect you, it also takes live video of you and places *you* within the game. This kind of technology was first used in games like Kung Fu High Impact, but with the Xbox One your video image is clearer than ever, and there's absolutely no lag.

Where Shape Up shines are in its mini-games, called "Challenges". They consist of different activities of about 2-4 minutes each that work out different parts of your body. In each game, you have a different challenge to meet, and you're pitted against an opponent whose video image you see to your right on the screen. Ingeniously, this opponent could be the system (which is technically a pre-recorded performance by the video trainer), an online opponent (who needs to be one of your Xbox Live friends), or even your own past performances or the past performance of someone who shares your Xbox with you. It's ingenious because as fun as the games are to play, it's even more fun when you're playing against someone else or even your own best performance.

Here's a synopsis of each game.

Arctic Punch - In this game, you're smashing ice blocks by punching, cross punching, and kneeing them. The ice blocks will show up in every direction around you, and there are certain times they'll rain down from above and you need to punch frantically. It's a great workout for your arms, legs, and abs. I give this one a workout value of 5 and a fun value of 4.

Knee Up Splash - This is like a cross between Fruit Ninja and the old game Simon. You need to memorize the patterns of red, green, and blue melons, and then grab the melons in the right order and smash them over your knee. At certain times a bunch of melons will come rolling toward you which you need to stomp with your feet. I give this a workout value of 4 and a fun value of 5.

Piano Step - This one is sort of a cross between Tom Hanks playing the piano in FAO Schwartz in "Big", Dance Dance Revolution, and Guitar Hero. Your task is to stomp your feet on one of four giant piano keys as the music plays and colored notes come speeding up toward you. There are a couple variations of this game to different songs (I Was Made for Lovin' You, Eye of the Tiger, Maniac, Lemonade Rush). Workout value of 5 and fun value of 5.

Abs Zapper - This one is sort of like Space Invaders or Galaxian. You lie on the ground, hold your hands together, and direct a laser beam to shoot oncoming aliens. Because of the position you're in, you end up getting a great core workout. Workout value of 5 and fun value of 4.

Squat Me to the Moon - Here, you just squat and stand, squat and stand. Do squats that are deep and quick, and you end up shooting yourself into outer space. You get a little break after a while of squatting when you can spread your arms out and shoot lasers at aliens. Workout value of 5 and fun value of 4.

Waterfall Jump - Another Guitar Hero-type game, this is basically a jump rope simulation, but to make it interesting you need to jump along to patterns that come racing at you. You get rewarded for accuracy as well as how well you jump. In between you hop on a surfboard and "surf" by leaning back and forth, trying to avoid mines. Workout value of 4 and fun value of 4.

Push Em Up - This is a game where you simply do push-ups. This is the one game where you Kinect definitely needs a very clear view of your floor. As you do pushups, your image on screen will have more and more things loaded onto its back from barrels to treasure cheese to arcade games to jeeps to elephants to killer whales. Workout value of 5 and fun value of 4 (thanks to the humor and entertainment value of what's happening on the screen). By the way, I haven't done a pushup in 15 years, which explains my abysmal performance.

Stunt Run - This is largely a duplicate of the "Runaway Train" game on Active Life: Explorer on the Wii, but of course greatly updated with more precise tracking and realistic graphics. Like Daniel Craig in Skyfall, you're running (in real life running in place) on the top of a speeding train and ducking and weaving to avoid obstacles, all while racing with the person to your right. This one has a workout value of 5 and fun value of 4.

To the Core - In this game you're "twisting" back and forth as you "drill" your way down to the depths of the ocean. You need to vary the speed and extent of your swiveling in order to navigate the depths, and from time to time you'll be able to "ride a vortex". Workout value of 4 and fun value of 3.

Volcano Skate - - In this game you simulate a speed skater to build up speed as you skate up a roller coaster track, and then once you're on top you simulate a downhill skier trying to avoid obstacles by lifting either foot or jumping. Workout value of 5 and fun value of 4.

What's great about all these games is that the "fun value" shoots up to 5 whenever you play against an opponent, whether that opponent is the computer, an online opponent, or your own past performance. You'll even be able to record a short video clip of yourself hamming it up to the cameras which will be displayed each time your performance is used by someone or yourself to compete against.

The game also supports local 2-person multiplayer, although bear in mind you're going to need a LOT of space between you and your opponent for most of the games, or you may end up knocking each other out. For single player, happily I was able to play most games with my Kinect only about 5 feet away from me (with the exception of Push 'Em Up, which required a full view of the floor).

Other than "Challenges", there's a section of the game called "Workouts" where your online instructors will take you through more traditional calisthenics and aerobic exercises of varying degrees of intensity. There's no gaming element to these, but you are rated based on how well you perform the exercise, and that all gets recorded in your overall stats.

There's also a story mode that'll take you through a 4 week regimen where each week you'll face off against a "champion". Three days a week for 15 minutes you'll need to complete a certain number of pre-selected workouts and challenges, with the goal of defeating your main challenger by the fourth week. It's a clever way to get you to play a number of the challenges and workouts together to maximize your fitness benefit, all while feeling like a video game "quest".

One of the nearest features of the game is its Statistics section, which shows you the number of "Bolts" (the game's point system) you've earned, as well as weekly statistics of the number of minutes you've worked out, the calories you've burned, and a percentage breakdown of which part of your body you've worked on (upper body, core, or lower body). There's also the ability to integrate with the "Shape Up Battle Run" app for iOS or Android to track your running or jogging statistics.

The one part I wasn't completely enthused about was the downloadable content. There's DLC called "Shape Up Coach" where you can create a customized workout program, choose one of 100 pre-made Training Quests, look up diet plans, and track your caloric intake to sync up with the game. At $24.99 a year, I personally don't think this is worth it, but for someone who perhaps loves this game to the point where he or she wants to build a diet and exercise plan around it, it might be.

Similarly, they're selling downloadable content called a "Shape Up Season Pass" that allows access to other Workouts and Challenges. My general attitude towards DLC is, it's fine to sell add-ons like new music tracks for the piano game or new exercises under Workouts, but when they're holding back core things like new Challenges from their packaged game and nickel-and-diming us to buy it as DLC, that admittedly rubs me the wrong way.

I'm happy to say I didn't experience any of the crashing or lagging that earlier reviewers had, so I'm assuming that they've since fixed these issues. I was able to play through all the Challenges with no problems at all.

Overall, I have to say that I was really impressed by this game. It truly is innovative in the way that it makes exercise fun. I won't go so far as to say this is the "killer app" that'll make the masses embrace the Kinect. But for those who still believe the Kinect has potential greater than just a curiosity or those of us in the niche of people who like to use video games for fitness and exercise, this is a truly innovative game that pushes the genre forward.
summary: One of the best exergames to date, providing an intense aerobic workout but being so fun you don't really notice nor mind.

As a fan of the RPG, button mashing, hack and slash I thought Lords of the Fallen would be right up my alley.  I've got to say, I'm happy with my purchase.

After only about 1:30 hours into the game, I can tell already is unlike so many of the hack and slash I've come to love and play:  Fable, Diablo, etc.  This game spews originality from the graphics to the lighting to the voice overs.  The graphics are big and bold, which is the first thing you will notice.  Your character, and the opponents you face are about 100X larger than a Diablo character and larger than the Fable ones and even Dragon Age Inquisition.

Decisions on what to carry, and equip play massively into how your character moves.  Energy is a HUGE component of this game as I learned while trying to defeat the first Lord for nearly an hour.

If you want a quick, out of my way, blood slashing RPG this ain't it.

But if you are willing to put your time in, you will be very pleased.

My only reason for the 4 stars is the difficulty which I've yet to find if you can change.
summary: Get ready for the long haul.

Singularity is one of those games (there are many) that is based on a great premise, but is executed in an unimaginative, derivative and disappointing fashion. The game is completely linear; there is nothing free-roam or exploratory to it. That's unfortunate, because it sure looks like a lot of time was spent on creating the environments. Would have been nice to be able to interact with them. Instead, you just proceed from one battle to the next. They might as well have put big yellow arrows on the ground. Some firefights are incredibly lopsided and difficult, until you figure out that one insider's secret that wins that particular battle. You run into bosses--another leftover device from the dark ages of FPS games (and one I wish developers would drop). Bosses are just a crutch, a way for developers not to make an interesting and unique game experience. Singularity ultimately falls flat and I can't overemphasize how quickly it became the worst thing a video game can become--uninteresting.

Cable is as pictured. Works with PSP 2000 & 3000 even know it does not state 3000. I have a 3000 and plugged it in as soon as I received it and it worked as expected. Also on the side of the package it says " works with PSP 2000/3000. I own a HDTV and noticed that the main menu of the psp appears full screen on the TV but when I start the game it reduced the size of the picture to basically a letterbox with big black borders around the edges. The only settings that can fix this are on only some T.Vs (enlarge picture settings). But hey, it's still a bigger picture. Of coarse with any small picture that you enlarge it will decrease the image quality a bit but it won't be so hard on your eyes. Unless you have a really small T.V. in which case I wouldn't even bother buying this cable. The cable is a decent length for the price (8.2ft.). This qualifies as a psp COMPONENT cable. Meaning 3 of the 5 prongs are for video (green, blue, red) 2 are for audio (red, white). The audio cables are also labeled audio on the cable right out of the package. Video will not work properly if you A. plug them into the wrong inputs and/or B. Leave out one of the video prongs. Do note that my TV has progressive scan and this cable notes that the TV needs progressive scan in order to work so I couldn't test this statement. Most HDTVs have progressive scan. other than that I am very pleased with this cable and recommend it if you are looking for a way to play your PSP 2000/3000 core system on T.V. when at home or a friends. Does not work with PSP 1000 or Vita. PS Vita doesn't even have a video out. They released the PS Vita T.V. in japan that lets you play your vita games on T.V.and more but unfortunately not here. At least not yet... Update 5/2/2014: If you have a standard T.V. with component in you may be able to get this cable to work by going to the connected display settings and setting the psp to "interlace" instead of "progressive". If you plug the psp into your standard T.V. with progressive setting set the screen will go crazy and it won't work no matter how many times you check the connections. I got my PSP to work on a standard TV this way. Most standard TVs do not support component though. Only A/V conections. I found a way to get the psp to display in full screen on HDTVs but unfortunately it requires a converter that will run you anywhere from 35-70$ here's a product link: Pyle Home PSPHD42 PSP to HDMI Converter with Upscaling for Portable Play Station If you look there may be a cheaper one that does the same job.

My very first Pokemon game was a copy of Pokemon Red that i received when I was in 6th grade back in 1999. I enjoyed that game wholeheartedly and I have pretty much owned every pokemon game since (with the exception of crystal and emerald). I am still biased in my loyalty to the earliest generations of Pokemon since that is what I grew up playing. I've had my favorites from the newer generations that I love to catch and use (Sceptile and Groudon from III, Garchomp from IV) but overall I feel that each generation of new pokemon seems like the designers are running short of good designs. However, Pokemon X and Y have blended old and new in addition to vastly improving the gameplay experience to make these games terrific.

First and foremost, I love the new 3D battle simulations. They really provide a more realistic experience to pokemon battles. Second, I love getting to see some of my favorite Pokemon from the early generations. I hated that Pokemon Black and White left all the old pokemon out until you beat the game, which is why those are my least favorite games. But X and Y really made up for it by allowing me to use my old favorites like Alakazam, Butterfree, and especially Charizard!

I haven't used some of the online trading features yet but I hear that they really enhance the gameplay experience and allow the ability to obtain all sorts of different pokemon to help complete the formidable task of completing the Pokedex.

Overall, I don't think I have ever enjoyed playing Pokemon this much before. Nintendo really hit a home run with these games. And upon hearing that they will be remaking Ruby and Sapphire with this generation, I am thrilled and hoping to experience these great new features on classic older games.

To make a long story short, DON'T WAIT!! BUY ONE OF THESE GAMES NOW!! Old and new players alike will find themselves addicted to the superior gameplay!!
summary: Unbelievable game! Easily the best Pokemon game generation so far!!

This was a great first entry in what wound up really being a great franchise. The story is somewhat basic, and the game play, in comparison with future titles, is a bit clunky and rough. The graphics don't stack up to future games. The weapons hadn't all been developed yet, the enemies hadn't either, and the fact of the matter is it was just a simpler time in the gears of war universe.

All those obvious facts aside, the game is still gears of war. It's tough as nails on higher difficulties, and has plenty of moments that are every bit as immersive as the rest of the franchise. If you play the other games, this one should be on your to do list and sooner than later. Solid game that shouldn't be missed if you're into gears at all.

Let me preface this by saying I really loved playing Fallout New Vegas and this review will compare it to Fallout 3 a lot and make some complaints. But that's because Fallout 3 was such an amazing experience that fallout the series is now held up to ridiculously high standards.

Fantastic game. However it always felt (and still does years later) like a really big DLC download that they should never have charged $ 60 for initially. The game was basically fallout 3 with a few tweaks that could have easily been implemented via patches to Fallout 3.

Aiming down the sights was great and much needed, making the gun fighting better and more FPS like. Being able to mod your guns was also a welcomed change.

The story line never felt as epic as Fallout 3's. It's ok but no where on the level of FO3.

I felt that the game could have given you more missions as a legionnaire. And speaking of factions, the game instead of focusing on karma like in FO3 focuses more on which factions you're friends with. So you could be a huge thieving, cannibalizing dirt bag with low karma but the NCR troops will be friendly towards you and say hi because you've attacked Ceaser's Legion. A silly oversight.

As for the DLC for this game, the Dead Money DLC is god awful. There's a mist that permeates large portions of the level and it automatically kills you. There's lots of other things that kill you and some holograms that are invincible. So no matter your level or skill, you'll be dying a lot the first time you go through it. I did and it was very frustrating. The worst is at the end....it's such a tease.

The other DLCS:
Honest Hearts- Not bad, no real interesting items, armor, outfits added
Old World Blues- Rather fun DLC, new interesting weapons, new perks, funny dialogue. This was probably by favorite DLC of the bunch. There's also a lot of upgrades and perks you can earn through this.
Lonesome Road- My second favorite of the DLC. Lots of new weapons and armor in this one. The story was a bit weak and a let down to me based on the build up and mystery of who the other courier was and his whole story etc.

Base game, plus 4 DLC packs and a greatest hits discount?  If you've never played this game get it.  It's hundreds of hours of entertainment for a super low cost.
I can't wait for Fallout 4.
summary: Hundreds of hours of entertainment for a low cost

reviewText:
When From Software first released Dark Souls II for the PC and last-gen consoles we here at Chalgyr's Game Room absolutely loved it. A near-perfect blend between action, brutally challenging gameplay, and traditional roleplaying elements, Dark Souls II was a strong contender for our Game of the Year awards, as well as being one of the titles that a few of us were quite thankful for in our 2014 Thanksgiving article. When we got wind of the remaster that was scheduled for release in April of this year for current-gen systems, and that it would contain the three DLC packs as well as some new content, we just had to get our hands on it and now that we have spent countless hours smiting hollows and dying (a lot), here is our review.

The most notable graphical upgrades come in the form of the framerate and lighting; while the lighting is not as nice as seen in the original E3 videos, it is a significant improvement of the original release. The framerate however, is absolutely glorious as it runs at 60 FPS with a few rare instances of FPS drop (most notably for me was while fighting the Old Iron King in the Iron Keep).

The new higher resolution textures are all clean and look great at 1080p and draw distances, where applicable, are as nice as ever. The one thing I did find that was quite annoying is that there are numerous places where, after killing a hollow, the item that they drop will be suspended above the actual body, I saw it most often in The Lost Bastille and The Iron Keep where it happened enough that it became more than a nuisance. Fortunately it seemed to be limited to just those areas, as it did not seem to happen in other locations with varying heights (like Huntsman's Copse and The Gutter). Beyond that one little issue, Scholar of the First Sin looks gorgeous on the PlayStation 4.

Gameplay has both changed and not changed. I have put about 250 hours or so into the original release and am currently on NG++++ (as I said, we here at Chalgyr's loved the game) so I went into Scholar of the First Sin thinking it would be a breeze. I could not have been more wrong. Enemy patterns and positions have changed significantly, throwing you up against some of the tougher enemies right out of the gate. Just as you enter the Forest of the Fallen Giants you are greeted by a couple of standard Hollow Infantry units and an Ogre.

With the puny weapons that you have access to (even the morningstar that is just off the cliff to Majula), taking on an Ogre and a Hollow Infantry or three is incredibly taxing. I died two or three times in that first mob until I removed my armor and simply went about dodging all over (I started as a Fighter, as I normally go Swordsman in a full Dex build, so I wanted to change things up) getting a hit in here or there to weed out the enemies. That first fight sets the pace for pretty much the entire game, as enemy counts are increased and terrible things, such as a Pursuer appearing at all of the wrong times, happen.

Boss fights seem largely untouched, though that could be because there are generally two summon signs (not including other player summons) outside of the mist door to the boss. If anything the access to more units make things quite easy. Take the Ruin Sentinels in The Lost Bastille; normally you would have the one NPC to help handle all three of them, but in Scholar of the First Sin you get a second NPC that can join, this one a melee character.

The melee character can soak up the damage and then the mage will pound them with Soul Spear (an immensely powerful spell). The two of them were able to take out two of the Sentinels before both were vanquished, leaving me just the third one to kill. While it is easy in the first run, I can only imagine (and hope) that Bonfire Aesthetics or NG+ will significantly increase the difficulty of the boss fights, as they seem to be the only thing that has not truly changed.

While the graphics and mobs got an overhaul, the music and sound effects did not. This is not a bad thing though, as the sound from the original game was absolutely wonderful. The audio conveys both a rich and gloomy atmosphere in Drangleic and the brilliance behind it can stir intense emotions like despair or dread, sadness and helplessness. For a game that can be downright brutal, the masterful audio is just another way for From Software to torment your soul.

Dark Souls II: Scholar of the First Sin, with its enhanced graphics, altered mobs, new content and the added DLC packs creates what is easily my favorite PlayStation 4 game to date. Between the updates that were made and the solid and wonderful gameplay, Dark Souls II is a must have for any current-gen console owners. Every defeated enemy is a victory unto its own and the payoff is well worth any bit of frustration that you may have with dying regularly.
summary: Worth going back and dying again

My initial review of The Sims 4 can be summed up in what I said to my daughter-in-law: When I get tired of designing new Sims and houses in The Sims 4 and actually want to *play* The Sims, I'll go back to The Sims 3, because actual gameplay in The Sims 4 is pretty much non-existent. Talk about boring nothing to do, that sums up actual gameplay for The Sims 4.

My thing is building skills, so I make Loner Sims. Nope, Loner doesn't work, my Sim is bombarded with people constantly cutting into the critical time she needs to complete career tasks or build other skills I'm interested in her acquiring. Apparently Loner in The Sims 4 just equates to "socially awkward" but never actually alone much. My Loner Sim finally took another Sim to raise, built a whole other floor onto her house and everything, then had her move in, just so my Sim could actually get some needed work done!

"Travel" to the house next door? Really?! Dig up a treasure thingy and get a goofy-looking statue you'd never put anywhere in your house? Really?! My Sim is also a Bookworm, but can I keep her on task reading a book? Nope. She needs to pee, does she head for the bathroom? Nope. Instead she autonomously starts out on a jog. Sheesh! Some things can't wait, girl! In The Sims 3 everything stops when it's bathroom time, that need bumps all the others, but apparently The Sims 3 Sims are a lot smarter than those in The Sims 4.

No object recolor... okay, I realize the textures load-time for The Sims 3 is a laggy and often buggy nightmare, so I'll concede on that; however, why is there no logic where comfort rating for furniture is concerned, even when choosing the same style but a loveseat instead of a chair? I like Modern style furniture, but if I want the most comfortable I must use the Victorian monstrosities. Apparently sleek lines paired with lush padding doesn't equate to comfort for The Sims 4 designers. The sleek modern-looking floor-standing stereo's fun rating isn't that high, the big clunky box of a stereo (that looks good nowhere and sticks out like a sore thumb when paired with the aforementioned Victorian monstrosities) is the high fun choice.

Thank goodness there's an option to revert to The Sims 3 style camera controls (increased functionality is not really a "revert" in my book), because The Sims 4 camera options truly suck. Why would they lock you into such limited viewing angles when The Sims 4 engine is perfectly capable of full 3D viewing like we're used to with The Sims 3?

You can put plants anywhere, outside, inside, on the deck, in a roof garden, I love that. Truly rounded corners (using the "Decks" tool) I love that. Maybe this game will grow on me... if I can just find some way to get away from all the other intrusive Sims and actually start to PLAY it. Maybe I haven't discovered the things that will make me love The Sims 4 yet for something other than its fantastic almost instantaneous load times. Until I've played a bit more, I'll reserve final judgment... if I could just have an actual LONER that would be a big plus.
summary: Underwhelmed right now, but reserving judgment

This is a really good remake.  I think this was a great remake of the originals and an improvement over X & Y.  Not that I hated X & Y.  In fact, those were the games that gave me a reason to buy a 3DS, but while those games were great, there were some small grips I had, grips that were fixed in Omega Ruby/Alpha Sapphire.  For starters, we don't have the skates that were in X & Y mapped to the Circle Pad.  Instead, the Circle Pad can be used for regular movement AND sneaking up on Poke'mon, which I believe improves the experience of collecting Poke'mon.  This game also introduces the exclusive Latios and Latias ability Soar, which is used with an item called an Eon Flute.  Not only did this let you find islands that are inaccessible by any other means, such as Mirage Islands and Spots, but I prefer using this ability over the HM Fly, because it makes the game feel more open-ended, and it doesn't use up a move slot.  Other than that, if you played the original games, you might notice some differences like the gym puzzles and layout, and Mauville City being a giant shopping mall with apartments, but most of everything is about the same.

I also feel like this game, along with the rest of the 3DS Poke'mon games, got some almost perfect trading systems.  Key word: Almost! The community can make you feel ripped off at times on the Wonder Trade.  I never expect to get legendary Poke'mon or shinies, though I have gotten lucky two times, but you should never expect to get anything like you're trading.  For example, let's say if you trade in a level 100 Lucario that you never used, there's a good chance you'll get a baby Poke'mon or something worthless like Bidoff or Magikarp.  I believe some thresholds should have helped out a bit, such as trading by rarity, level, etc., but with it being random, there is a small chance that you can get lucky by trading worthless Poke'mon.  If you are thinking about using one of the trade systems, other than direct trade, I say the GTS is a better choice, but even that's not always perfect.  You do see crazy trade deals, such as giving a level 1 Skitty for a Groudon that's in the level range of 91-100, and some would be asking for event Poke'mon, such as Diance.  These were probably kids who don't know better, but it's not all lost hope, as long as you can make trades that make sense.  I don't feel like the trade systems are a massive flaw of the game, as they are mostly community ran, but of course, I do think these systems would have been great features, instead of ones I'm scared of using.

Other than that, I can't really find any faults with the remake.  You can't always play it in 3-D, and the 3-D does make the frame drop in battles, might be too taxing on the system when it already looks great in those moments, but I don't think many people who own the system use the 3-D 24/7 anyway.  I recommend this game to anybody, including people who played the original, as this game is a great remake and an improvement over the last Poke'mon.

reviewText:
This is definitely a must-have accessory for your Wii U if you are a huge Smash Bros fan. You simply cannot beat the controller experience of a classic GameCube controller in that game. It also works in other games, but Smash is without a doubt the priority here, since most other games I'm more easily able to adjust to different controllers.

These are starting to be much more easily purchasable from major retailers, so I would highly recommend against paying more than MSRP for one of these and supporting scalpers. I finally, after several months of waiting, was able to snag one from amazon and couldn't be happier. Just be patient and you'll definitely be able to get one more easily as time passes.

I even tried some alternatives while waiting for the official adapter. I bought several Hori Battle Pads and PDP Fight Pads. These are GameCube-style controllers that plug into your Wii remotes, which makes them semi-wireless. They worked well and were certainly an upgrade from using the Wii U gamepad, Wii remote, or even the pro controller, but were still not quite as good as the real deal. Finally, with this adapter, I can use my actual GameCube controller and classic control scheme I'm used to, and finally with rumble too!

The third party adapters seem to be an acceptable replacement if you're too impatient to wait for an official one. I haven't tried them all, but I did try one third-party GameCube adapter and it looked and functioned identically to the Nintendo brand adapter. It only was missing the Wii U logo on it, and didn't have as pretty of a box (no big deal).

So while this accessory is king, don't be willing to pay more than necessary for it, as there is a larger stock of these on its way to retailers, and plenty of third-party options that will also allow you to use GameCube style controls on your Wii U.
summary: A must-have! But there are plenty of alternatives, so *don't* pay over MSRP for one!

reviewText:
So today, my dad helped pay for some of this. I bought it digital, so it's built into my Wii U system. I honestly think this game is great! Here is what I have to say about Splatoon.

-Story Mode (1 player)
I think the adventure is great, but at times it feels, I dunno "empty" I guess. It's hard to explain but if you buy this, you'll understand. I think the story is great. It's simple, Squids=good, Octarians=bad. You must save the stolen Zapfish. It's very fun.

-Turf War (online mode, 1 player)
I honestly L-O-V-E this mode! It's super fun to try to cover over the other team's territory. There are several stages and weapons to play in/with. The only problem is, the servers are terrible! My Dad says it doesn't work very well because there are to many people trying to play at once, since it just came out 2 weeks ago. But besides that I think it's amazINK.

-Battle Dojo (2 players)
The Battle Dojo is a fun 2-player mode. I have never played it, ands you'll find out why. Basically, there are just 2 players, popping as much as balloons as possible. Simple concept. I was EXTREMELY disappointed to find out you have to have a stinkin' Pro Controller ($45, btw) to play multiplayer! I'm very disappointed in Nintendo's "wise" decision. So, it's very sad, but I've heard its great fun.

-Customization
I have to say this was a great idea to add in. Everyday, New clothes, shoes, hats, etc. show up at the shops. That gets me giddy, because you wonder, could my favorite piece of clothing be at the store today? I love choosing your own weapons an outfits!!

Well, I'd say that about sums it. There are also many other fun surprises I didn't cover. I personally think this game is perfect, besides the communication errors ALL THE TIME! If Nintendo can fix that, man best game ever right here folks! Also Nintendo comes out with free  DLCs just about every week, that's cool too!

Hope this helped!

The Just Cause series is one that came out of nowhere for me. Several years ago, I heard about Just Cause 2 from its small following, and they only had amazing things to say, such as how it was what a sandbox game should be and that everything was destructible and traversable. Generally speaking, they were right. Not everything is destructible; in fact, little is, but much more is destructible here than in most sandbox games. And, as advertised, everything was traversable and it's one of the best sandbox games I've ever played. I still have no idea when or for what the first entry in the series was released, but I never really cared and I still don't. Needless to say, this is why I even know about Just Cause 3 and why I now own it.

Nothing has really changed in Just Cause 3 from 2, and that's a good thing. You can still destroy everything (read: a lot), everything can be traversed, and it's still far better than most sandbox games out there. That being said, its same flaws are back as well. Challenges are, to me, pointless, and the game does get very boring and repetitive after a few hours since everything is exactly the same: find a new area to destroy stuff and kill people in until it's conquered. Then repeat, again and again. The island is much bigger this time around and the map contains several smaller ones, and while it's amazing to see, it makes the repetitive and hollow nature of the game even more apparent.  All the same mechanics are here from the last game to keep everything together, but for some reason, it feels much more difficult to get around in this one. When using the grappling hook or parachute, I find myself stumbling a lot more, sometimes to the point where my character will practically be flung about as a result, and is almost always killed or taken back a few steps from it. It's also common to grapple a surface area and be pulled into an impassible one for several seconds before the grappling stops and lets you fall. This could be because of the ability to, essentially, move however you want, but it feels broken, like it's glitching or the different features aren't as compatible or smooth as they were in the last game. I found this glitching to be more and more prevalent as I played, growing from an occasional occurrence to a semi-regular annoyance. For being a game based around destruction, I'm a bit disappointed with despawn rates as well. Destroyed and otherwise tampered with vehicles, people, and bits of scenery despawn by being just a few yards away from them. As an example, if on a road, just walking down it from an area where destruction took place and turning around will have already made everything disappear. While despawns need to and should happen, the radius of them is very small. I've also noticed that, in any given area, only about 20 vehicles, destroyed or working, can be present at a time before new ones stop driving by. This very much limits the ability to actually play in a sandbox as big as this one is. And, like other reviews mention, load times are fairly long, but after having just played Fallout 4, which has load times that are regularly double the length of these, I barely notice them, so it's hard to knock the game down that much for them. The worst thing, though, is the constant attempt the game makes to connect to the internet. Regularly, pausing the game and going to the map will prompt the game to either check for a connection or say it has lost a connection. Despite having put the game in offline mode numerous times, this continues to frequently happen, the events sometimes lasting longer than the load times do.

In the end, I'm torn between a 3 and 4 star rating. It's a Just Cause game. You grapple and parachute around while blowing up the landscape around you, forgetting there was a plot at all and wising you had when you remembered there was one. And it delivers in that. But, being that it does get repetitive and is held back by glitches, load times, and an unneeded and unwanted online feature, I feel a 4 star rating is just a bit too high. 3 1/2 stars would do, but being Amazon, that means it can only get a 3 from me. I'd definitely recommend this game, but it's not a must have at full price.

This game is fantastic. Easy Recommendation. If you enjoy car games and car sims specifically, this is a don't miss game.

Great game. Like ALL other sports games, last year's version will suit you fine since there's nothing monumental here to make it worth $60 (the back cover advertises licensed equipment, seasonal sun paths and player personalities. Who cares???) However, if you want the newest version and have the money to spare then you obviously have no other choice.

Game Play
It looks awesome. I'm not sure if 14 was 60 FPS, but I believe that 15 is. The game still looks awesome and the physics are great. I don't notice a huge difference between 14 and 15 though which is expected from sports games. The crowd does look better though, but not really that important.

Fielding
Fielding in the outfield is the hardest I've ever seen in a baseball game. It's not super hard, but you have to pay attention and be more accurate than before. Where there used to be a large circle around your player that you had to stand in to catch the ball, the circle now is just barely bigger than your player. Not very forgiving. You will throw a lot of controllers if you are not careful. Infield is basically the same as every baseball game I've played since 2005 at least.

Pitching
This is clearly the biggest change from 14. First, the pitch meter changed. It's now like the kick meter in Madden instead of that stupid circle that got bigger and smaller. I hope whoever created that was fired. I always hated it. It's a lot easier to throw perfect pitches. And your stud pitchers aren't out of gas in the 4th inning after throwing only 50 pitches. Also, they got rid of the arrows that showed how the pitch moves which I never found to be accurate anyways. Now it shows the entire trajectory of the ball from your hand to home plate. I think it is way more accurate this year. I remember so many times in 14 the pitch was not anywhere near (or completely opposite of) where it should have gone. Now if you throw a perfect pitch it goes where you want it. One thing I do not like is how your pitcher freaks out just by having one guy on base. The meter literally goes like 10x faster. Obviously the pitcher would be slightly more uncomfortable but it seems like the pitcher treats one guy getting on base like it's 0-0 in the bottom of the 9th of game 7 of the world series with a full count and the bases loaded.

Hitting
It seems basically the same as 14. Rarely will you strike out, but most of the time when you hit the ball (even crush it) it's always right at someone or some pathetic 3 foot dribbler. I hardly ever get more than about 5-7 hits a game. Maybe the hitting algorithm takes into account how many pitches you take, because I don't take a lot. I don't have 2 hours to play one game.

All this considered, if you are someone that gets the newest sports game every year, then you probably will this year too and you will not be disappointed.

reviewText:
It's cute, and sorta fun to play in 2-player with your kid (I play as Woodstock to help take out the baddies).  But the game has some pretty big design problems.  It's fairly average as a platformer, but the mechanics are a bit unresponsive and "floaty".

The most glaring issue that I have with this game is probably just an issue with this particular port of the game.  It doesn't feel like the controls were properly adjusted when it was play tested.  The XBox 360 analog stick, when pressed very gently to the left or right, does not always return to dead center.  A properly programmed game would likely ignore this "dead zone", but this game registers even the slightest variance in the X-Axis and Snoopy keeps running forever.  I assure you that it is only an issue with this game as it was tested with multiple controllers.  It just seems to be a third-rate port of a game that could be decent with a bit of minor patching.

Furthermore, the game isn't really properly tweaked for either seasoned gamers or beginners.  It's too easy for seasoned gamers and they will quickly become annoyed with the broken platforming mechanics.  It's too hard for beginners, quite possibly because of the broken mechanics but also due to early levels that can be frustrating at first for young children.  It's just unbalanced and doesn't target either type of gamer correctly.  I've seen posts from other reviewers that say it's both too hard and too easy.  There is a good reason for this.

Lastly, there is no manual at all.  I know that the days of manuals are largely gone from games, but this one is the worst offender I've seen yet.  Rather than having a manual in the game itself, there is some very small text on the printed insert that tells you to go to Activision's site for instructions.  I know it's not a challenging game (I haven't even died once yet and can keep most enemies at bay from my son with Woodstock) but there are some legit questions about the gameplay that are not explained by Charlie Brown's weak tutorials when you stumble upon something new.

The printed insert's manual address is https://support.activision.com/manuals , but if you click on one of the "Peanuts" links, all it does is provide you with a license agreement for the game alone with some warranty information.  Even more annoying is that it is in MS Docx format.  Activision is clearly clueless.

It's good as a $10 game.  Maybe a $15 game.  $20 or more is pushing it.  It's clearly a cheap cash-in on the Peanuts property to coincide with the new movie.  Granted, you can expect as much with a licensed game but it would have been good for Activision to put just a bit more work into this.  They're quickly becoming one of my most disliked game developers / publishers along with EA and SEGA.  Companies that used to be great back in the era 8-16-bit game machines.

Wait this one out until it's a little less expensive.
summary: Cute, But Not Without a Lot of Problems.

reviewText:
While this is a second installment to the franchise, it really merely feels like a continuation of the first. Now there is a solo quest line that can garner coins and progression, but even still it doesn't quite have a sense that you are really accomplishing much in the process other than accumulating more coinage.

The multiplayer, while improved (albeit the bar was not set very high by the 1st anyway) still leaves little to be desired. Constant disconnects are prevalent and you and part of your group even get placed on separate teams at times when matches are set up. That makes zero sense to me. Why would you want to be in a group with your friends only to be paired up on separate teams?!

Again I state this really just seems like a continuation of the previous game instead of its own, separate sequel. Glorified DLC comes to mind often as I've played it which leads me to think it is not near worth the full price tag of a stand alone game but more worthy of arcade pricing around $30 or so. I would recommend waiting for it to drop in price to purchase.

Lastly the new stuff. There are new maps of course, but really it is as though you play the same 3 over and over so I truly can't tell how many more there really are (again..... glorified DLC). Also new characters were added to the original lot. They all have some merit and are a neat addition where each has something unique to bring to the table. Unfortunately though the characters are very uneven with regard to attack and ability. The Rose is especially one of these characters where a player simply holds down RT the whole time, darting back and forth, able to get "vanquishes" with great frequency. I would consider it the most "cheap" or "cheesed" character of them all. The disconnect and even feel with the character classes tells me they simply used the Beta as a hype builder more so than a way for gamers to give input for improvements of the game.

All in all this is merely an ok "expansion" to the GW universe. The map design should have really gone more so toward the less is more than the cluttered mess they turn out to be. The hit detection is fairly poor at times and frame rate dropping should not be happening in this day and age of gaming with such a simple game. But once the price drops about half this game will be worth a buy imo.
summary: A sequel..... but really only in name.

I write this review partly as a public service. I see a lot of people are writing reviews saying that they are not audiophile. I thought maybe I could help shed some light on just how good the sound on these headphones is, because I definitely am an audiophile (literally translated "sound-lover"). No, I don't own vacuum tube pre-amps, or even a surround sound receiver. I don't have a power regulator hooked up to my refrigerator to keep it from putting distortion on the sound from my record player. But I HAVE received a ton of headphones good and bad (to the point where I often refer to myself as the "headphone king" these days. I just love great headphones, and I'm not ashamed of it.

I came across this gaming headset when I visited my family yesterday. My sister (non-gamer) told me that she was shocked by the quality of this headset, and so was my father when they tested it out. Like me, they often get items like this for free in exchange for reviews. Being the resident headphone expert, I took one look at this set and said "No way. I don't want to touch them!" They looked expensive, and quite frankly, like a set of studio monitors that sell in the hundreds of dollars. I didn't want to tempt myself with something I couldn't afford. But of course eventually I couldn't resist and had to test them.. you know, for science!

OH MY GOD

Okay, I hooked these up to my iPhone and ran my usual battery of tests. I ran the Tron Legacy Overture, some pieces by the Prague Philharmonic, and then moved on to some classic rock, broadway pop, and the Interstellar, and Man of Steel soundtracks. First of all, these are the most comfortable headphones of this style I have ever encountered. They are incredibly soft and fit nicely over your ears and head. They are a tad heavy and a tad tight, but they are brand new so I figure the fit will adjust slightly over time. As for the weight, well you are going to want to listen to these sitting down comfortably anyway.

People often say things like "I heard details I never heard before." and "the sound was full and rich", which tells you absolutely nothing about the real quality of headphones. These headphones, this headset... This is actually difficult for me to describe but I'll try.

I cried. So did my sister

Every track I listened to, I found myself closing my eyes and concentrating on the sound, becoming absorbed in it, lost in it. Listening to "No Time For Caution" from Interstellar in it's entirety, I felt like I was in the theater SEEING the film. No more than that. The sound was better than the theater. I opened my eyes, there were tears streaming down my face. I wiped them away. More tears came. I was having an EMOTIONAL reaction to the music I was listening to. The song finished. I turned my iPhone to the conclusion of the track "Where We're Going" and had my sister listen. I was shaking with emotion for minutes after what I had heard. I had literally NEVER had that strong an emotion reaction to listening to music in my entire life. I looked at my sister as she handed the headphones back. She was wiping away tears.

Are you okay?

"These headphones...I've never seen that movie, but these headphones...I felt like I was watching the movie." Yeah that's what she said. She had an emotional reaction too. I ran more tests. My low fidelity recording of Magic Knight Rayearth's orchestral theme was a bit fuzzy, and staticy as expected, but it as much more "alive" than many other headphones could render, and less flat. I listened to Queen's "Who Wants to Live Forever" and felt like I had the orchestra all around me. Every rap of the symbol, every slide of the guitar strings was so REAL. As my sister was showing me some of Josh Groban's "Stages" on YouTube through the horrible TV speakers that evening, I decided to listen to "Dulcinea". Wow. A high definition recording absolutely EXPLODES on these headphones, with incredible clarity, power, and resonance.

When you put this headset on, everything disappears but the music. The sound stage is so complete you just feel like you are THERE when listening to a good recording. The noise isolation is "passive" so basically the thick padding blocks out some noise. You can still kind of head people outside if they are trying to get your attention (which is kind of a good thing). On the other hand, sound leakage is virtually non-existent. There's some static when you adjust the volume using the knob, and I never turned it up to max because I want to keep my hearing.

Gaming Headset

I'll be absolutely honest. I've tested headsets by Turtle Beach (Garbage), Monster ($300.00 for a $100.00 headset), etc, and generally speaking manufacturers appear to be ripping off gamers who they know can't tell good sound from bad and good workmanship form cheap junk. These headphones are more like monitors than a gaming headset. They have a weaved cord, HEAVY padding both for the ears and the head, and are made with aluminum frames, not plastic (I've had plastic frames break before).

Sure, you could use these for a gaming headset, but to use them ONLY for gaming would be absolutely idiotic. Yeah, they are a little lacking in bass, they are a little expensive (I'll be honest, these are easily worth $400.00), but they are PERFECTLY balanced. Their sound-stage is impeccable. They are smooth and tight across the full range of sound. Strings shine, vocals soar, detail is exquisite. They are quite simply the finest sounding headphones it has ever been my privilege to test, and I ordered a pair the same night I tested them even thought I already have many headphones and I had to pay full price.

Seriously? A GAMING HEADSET???

To be honest, I'm kind of amazed these are marketed as a gaming headset. Gamers as a whole are NOT going to understand what they have in this headset. For gaming use, they may actually be a bit lacking (because heavy bass is going to be more important in shooters and action films), but as a set of headphones these are shockingly good.

If you buy a pair of headphones better than these, you are going to need to address the other bottlenecks and invest in a high quality stereo, pre-amp, DAC, etc (and sky's the limit on how much that can cost). For regular listening without all the extras I consider these the pinnacle. If you are an audiophile on a budget or just want a pair of headphones for serious listening, buy these, plug them into your phone, close your eyes, and soar into heaven.

* Update 02/01/16*

As promised I finally got around to some "gaming" testing of this headset and I have come to some interesting conclusions. It appears that other reviewers are indeed correct that this set has low volume and even poor quality under some circumstances. It's not really an issue, but rather a characteristic.

This headset needs a lot of power to drive it. My iPhone sounds great. My laptop likewise sounds incredible. But when I tried to hook them up to my living room stereo amp...

Ugh!

Yeah, Halo sounded atrocious through this set. To be fair, it's a cheap stereo amp and the signal through the receiver is combined from my desktop PC and Xbox 360. I had to turn the sound way up on the receiver, and it just sounded poor. Sound was scratchy and distorted. Likewise the sound quality from my PC was bad.

I tried running this set directly from my PC and the sound was REALLY low, unacceptably so.

Conclusion

This is a GREAT set of headphones, but it is horribly mis-marketed/targeted. It SHOULD be sold as serious music listening headphones that require a pre-amp or amplifier. As a gaming headset I'm calling this product a FAIL. The Monster Gaming headset I reviewed a while back that cost double this one's price had worse clarity, was unbalanced, and had static and drop-out of sound. It also amplified the sound of a 360 sufficiently to play games like Halo.

Creative should bundle this headset with an external amplifier like Turtle Beach etc do with their sets. As a console gaming headset this item is NOT recommended. If like me, you have an iPhone or a laptop with very powerful amplification (it works amazingly on my laptop) then this is your headset.

As a last note of fairness to Creative, I believe they are EXPECTING you to use a headphone amplifier with this headset.
summary: The Best Pair of Headphones I Have Ever Tested. Requires external headphone amplifier for console gaming.

reviewText: It is what it is, got it free with Bing reward gift card.

I also have the Logitech G633 Artemis Spectrum and will focus on comparing these two headsets.  As I write this, the Man OWar is priced at $169.99, while the Logitech G633 is $129.99 (with the wireless version, the G933, at $169.99).

With the Artemis, having the ability to have two audio connections is fantastic!  Having a USB connection to speak in game and a 3.5 mm connection (for a phone, music device, etc.) is really, really nice.  The Artemis is more secure on my head.  The Man OWar fits a bit loosely and rapid movement can cause it to slip.  This may be because I am short and it won't adjust to my head well.  Surely, the Man OWar would not fit children well, but the Artemis will.

My Logitech is wired, but here Ill compare the Logitech G933, which is the wireless version of the Logitech I have.  Both the Razer Man OWar and the Logitech G933  use 2.4 GHz wireless technology.  Game time between charging is better with the Man OWar.  For the Man OWar you get 20 hours (14 hours with the Chroma lighting); for the Artemis you get 12 hours (8 hours with the fancy lights on).

The Man OWar uses Razer Surround, while the Artemis has both Dolby and DTS Headphone:X surround options.  Some people prefer Razer Surround and some people dont.  Which you like may well depend on the games you play. According to Razer, Using state-of-the-art technology, the surround sound algorithms powering Razer Surround creates an unbelievably accurate surround sound environment and allows gamers to calibrate positional sound to match their own unique preferences. This level of calibration allows Razer Surround to provide an incredible level of precision in-game  Thus, if you do a lot of shooter games, Razer Surround may suit you better.  Do note that you can download Razer Surround and use it with any headphones; there is a free version and a Pro version that you have to buy.  Overall, I prefer Razer Surround for gaming, but not for music.

My favorite is the Logitech Artemis because it is more secure on my head and I really love the 3.5mm connection on the Artemis. The Razer Man OWar still rates 5 stars because it is offers a high quality experience and great wireless range.

These headphones are very close and some will find the Man OWar to be their favorite.
summary: Man OWar vs. Logitech Artemis Spectrum

Got to 91% before I said screw it i got other games to play

First some background info. I've played the entire game series from the start of the original Naruto: Ultimate Ninja Storm. I've also played the side games Naruto: Ultimate Ninja Storm Revelations and Generations. I terms of watching the anime; I  watched the entire original series and dropped off after a season and a half of Shippuden since I was in college at the time and was too lazy to get catch up. The video games provided me with a great summery of the plot though

This is a free roam arena battle style game. Every character has the same basic control scheme so you don't have to worry about memorizing character specific move sets. The roster totals over 100 and the reason to choose one over the other is how cool the characters and specials look, and speed relative to attack/defense (or just choose deidara the most OP character in the game). This makes it a more psychological game as you have to know when to defend, attack and dodge while keeping track on the yours and your enemies energy bar. At high levels of play it's more of a focused on twitch gameplay than everything else as you have to press the button at the right time to counter or dodge the enemy before they land a devastating combo on you.

New to the battle system is that you can swap between characters as well as have them as support. You do share the same life bar so you cannot use a character to the last inch of health then switch character last minute to save you from losing the match like in the Capcom vs games.

The visuals are very good like previous games having an anime look to it. The downside I guess is that seeing that the game is now on the PS4 it doesn't look much improved since the last PS3 game and they could have had it on both PS3 and PS4. The framerate on the the other hand is very smooth even when there's tons of randomly explosions and flashes on screen. Cutscenes are okay; they are more visually appealing than the last game, but they either got lazy or pressed for time because there's a lot of still frame shots which is a bit disappointing.

Story is very good giving different perspective on varying characters instead of just follow Naruto. Also with the story taking place between less than half a dozen locations, seeing this, the story mode is no longer open world rather you have a selectable branching path level scheme similar to the 2D Mario series level select screen. If you miss the open world level traversal like in previous games there's Adventure mode where you are able to wander around the village and do side/fetch quests for collectables. The story of adventure mode surrounds the relationship between Hinata and Naruto for the most part. As for the main story mode, if you never played the past titles or not familiar with the anime you will get lost on what's happening. You do get flash back sequences, but only if you do all the QTE (quick timed events) correctly. If you only play the games and missed out on the anime like I have then you can piece together most ofo the story from the plot, but it seems like some of the details are missing as the story only tread on major plot points. Story mode is very easy to play through, if you lose a match the game presents you with full health power up and a choice between a defense or attack boost then puts you right where you left off where you fainted in the game. Retrying does affect your score though so if you want to S rank (especially for trophies) best to be on top of your game.

There are collectables in the game which gives you cosmetic items for characters and items for your online profile

Online is a step down. There's no spectator mode and online lobbies only have 4 people instead of 8 compared to the last game.
Trophies are easy to get, most revolve around getting S rank in story mode missions where in order to get it you have to satisfy battle conditions set prior to each match let it be win with 50% health or connect with combination secret technique at least one time in match. Matches are easy to get into as of this review as there's a lot of players online. If you see someone playing as deidara you know they're more into the win than the fun factor.

Overall aside from the character switching there's not much advancement to the battle system, it's a decent conclusion to the series but didn't go out with a bang in my opinion. Good if you want to catch up on the story without watching all the filler
