I have been playing NBA 2K and basketball video games in general my whole life, my first video game ever was "Basketball" on the Atari 2600 in 1978, I've played every NBA Live and 2K game ever in existence. I'll start this review off by saying that this game isn't terrible and it isn't completely broken. It had great potential. The graphics are great... and that's really about it for the reason why I gave this game 2 stars and not 1. However, I would honestly give this game a 1.5 or even a 1.25.

The storyline in MyCareer did not live up to the hype, you couldn't control anything in the story other than which high school and college Freq went to, which doesn't have any real effect on the storyline. This was definitely not one of Spike Lee's finest works, and I loved "Do the Right Thing" and "He Got Game". The whole story was clich, stereotypical, predictable, and I felt no connection to "Frequency Vibrations" (one of the worst names I have ever heard in any sort of narrative). Even after it is over, the storyline and the legacy of Victor Van Lier (Freq's self-centered, reckless late best friend who proceeds to blackmail him due to Dirtbike Donnie and ruins his entire rookie year due to him being the sole reason why Freq was cut by the team's general manager) whose death continues to linger and overshadow the rest of Frequency Vibration's career.

Most connections are useless as most of them give you useless jerseys to hang in your court (except for Michael Jordan and Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, who respectively give you your own Terminal 23 court and Kareem goggles).

Gameplay is practically broken in MyCareer as your teammates and your player consistently miss wide open three-pointers, jump shots, layups, and dunks no matter their rating while the CPU will mercilessly knock down a very high percentage of contested threes, jumpers, layups, and dunks, again, no matter their rating. If you take a contested three, jumper, layup, or dunk, forget about it, you are nearly guaranteed to miss it every single time, no matter your rating if you are the player. This is what you get if you play on Hall of Fame difficulty. I once saw Julius Randle miss an open dunk on the fast break with no one around because he hung on the rim and the ball rattled out on the way in. It was so frustrating. This game is nearly unplayable with the amount of frustration, rage, anger, fury, and disappointment it makes you feel while playing.

The animations around the rim are just terrible, and contact dunks were much better in 2K14 and 15. There is no fun when playing this game anymore.

I enjoyed 2K14 much more than this, and their lighting was much better than this game. I even enjoyed it's Park better than this, because at least players didn't spam signature styles and such.

The NBA 2K16 MyPark community is cluttered with players looking to exploit the game at any chance they get, with glitches, bugs, spamming size up animations, and all that. It's just dreadful. It is much more fun for me to play Pro-Am on NBA Live 16 than MyPark or Pro-Am in 2K16 because I can actually get into a game and enjoy it without terrible animations and players spamming the Jamal Crawford Inside Out animation. I can't stand it.

MyGM was pretty good but it could definitely still use a lot of improvement. I'm short on time so I'll just say that a YouTuber named Agent 00 made a video discussing this topic.

If I were to give 2K for 2K17 some suggestions, fix the gameplay, let the player customize their MyCareer, let it last for more than 1 season and AT LEAST 3-5 seasons as that would be awesome. Fix the animations, let the player make open threes, jumpers, layups, and dunks, and don't let the CPU always make contested threes, jumpers, layups, and dunks at a high rate. By the way, give us more customization options of haristyles and beards in MyCareer, we want designs from 2K14 back!! We want to have nice lineups like Live 16 and nice fades. We want parts in our hair and lines on the side. We want the Odell Beckham Jr hairstyle that Allen Crabbe has in real life, or at least give us a mohawk fade or a mohawk afro fade please.

PLEASE. Make 2K17 better than this. PLEASE.
summary: Here's hoping that 2K17 will be much better...

reviewText:
First off this is a game you NEED to play because it represents a milestone in video game technology. Hands down, one of the most visually stunning ever created. That said, most of the 3 star reviews are dead on and the most accurate.

Pros:

Various and fun gunplay
Beautiful graphics
Great voice overs and actors
Engaging enough story
You end up caring about Gallahads fate and where it leads him

Cons:

Slow unbearable at times walking everywhere (not being able to control the speed of your character at all times is frustrating beyond belief)
Can't shoot or attack at random. You can only fight when the game says you can fight. Another extreme draw back.
Can't explore (although to be fair, this game wan't meant to be some open world Fallout 4)
Last boss battle was a Quick Time Event. It sucked.

Biggest complaint:

TERRIBLE ENDING for the investment. It took about a month for me to beat the game because A) I have other things to do and B) I play other games. But for such a good build up the ending just leaves you going...uh? Granted there is a small cut scene after the credits that hints at a possible sequel but you get no resolution from any of the characters except G.

My biggest thing as a gamer was always the ending. Sure gameplay is #1  but the ending should be #2. I think Bioshock Infinite ruined my expectations for endings of games now lol I always expect some giant resolution.

All in all you would be remiss if you didn't experience this game ONCE. It doesn't deserve anything less than 3 stars if not for the graphics and well thought out story and characters. Any reviews less than 3 are just amplifying disgust over the cons I've already mentioned.

Try it out and experience. It's cheap enough now.
summary: Must play. No matter the reviews.

I bought this with one purpose: Replace a stylus that is no longer staying place in my New 3DS XL. Telescoping was a bonus because I was wondering if anyone would ever produce such things for the newer systems.

This came in a cute little baby blue package. Bit of a pain to open to but the results are what matter: It DOES fit in a New 3DS XLThe one with the new buttons and C Stick, yeah that one. (Because of confusion) And yes, it comes with all the colors/quantities displayed in the picture.

Here's the catch though: The notch protruding out of the upper part that keeps it locked in is a little larger than a regular one's would be. So it will fit, but some pressure needs to applied to get it in or it needs to be filed down ever so carefully. In which case regular use will probably wear it down to the point where it would fit in easily. This also probably explains why my current one slips out because the notch is worn down. I didn't file it down, nor would I recommend it unless you're willing to sacrifice a stylus.

As for the telescoping, it's a little stiff/rough. In which case, again, extensive use probably will break it in and make things smoother.
summary: It fits so I can't really knock it. Telescoping bonus points.

Fantastic collection of games. Whether playing for nostalgia, or jumping in for the first time, these classics are insanely enjoyable. Especially Conker and Banjo.

After reading about the awards/nominations this game received, along with the stunning visuals, I took advantage of a sale price and bought it. I'm new to the Witcher series, so I was concerned about having to know the "lore". While there's lots to learn, it's not impossible to start with this version. I enjoy games like Fallout, Just Cause, etc., but not so much into fantasy-themed games (creating potions, etc. just isn't in my interest).

That being said, there is a lot to learn in terms of beefing up your character. It is incredibly easy to die and you need to remember to save a lot. Just stumbling down a hit will ping your health level. Yes, I'm still trying to earn enough $ to beef up my character, but many side missions are tough to complete without sufficient weapons/armor. When you die, re-spawning takes a ridiculous amount of time. I'd also like to point out that the size of text displayed in the game's interface is really tiny. Perhaps it is my aging eyes, but I've never had to strain as much to read text as I do in this game...and there doesn't appear to be an adjustment setting.

I will say that there is an absolute TON of things you can do in this game, beyond the main missions. But I will also point out the formulaic structure of each primary mission gets to be repetitive at times. It seems whomever you meet up with has info to help you on your quest, but won't divulge until you do a separate mission/favor for them. Too predictable.

Overall, I actually do like this game. Once my character gets sufficiently equipped and protected, I'll perk up a bit. It's just tough sledding early on. I hope the developers find a way to speed up re-spawning times. No other game takes this long...not even close.
summary: Enjoyable, tons of gameplay a plus, but with faults

Ever wonder what it would be like to be the CEO of a mega corporation? Weve all had those dreams of wearing the $9000 suit with your Italian leather shod feet resting comfortably on your custom mahogany desk you flew in from some jungle, a snifter of rare whiskey resting next to them as you sit back contemplating how awesome you are. Its the American dream (and to a degree the Canadian dream as well) to be so rich that you only have to worry about building another garage for your high powered sports car collection. Now imagine you could take that company almost global, heck, you could take it the moon, literally! Anno 2205 lets you live that solidly corporatist, material dream of owning a chunk of the planet with more money than you know what to do with, while competing with other mega corporations to be top dog and helping poor people when it suits you.

Its an interesting mix of economy/city builder, real time strategy combat, and story driven missions.  You play as a promising global corporation that gets selected to be involved in the Lunar Licensing program which aims to build a fusion reactor on the moon to pipe power back down to the earth. The three phase program involves building up an economic stronghold in a temperate zone, then expanding into the arctic and eventually onto the moon itself to acquire the rare resources available in each different biome and complete the fusion reactor. Your employees are your main source of income and the higher the level your workers are the more money they provide you. In turn, each promotion level requires you to satisfy their base, advanced and luxury needs, forcing you to expand your production into increasingly complex goods. Each biome provides unique resources needed to advance your employees making trade between the sections, and the world market via the space station, essential.

While you're building up each section you can complete various tasks for rare goods used to upgrade your factories. There are also section objectives available, such as repairing an ancient dam or creating Arctic sea routes that provide large bonuses in power or resources. These quests are your typical fetch and carry requests that make you wonder just how lazy these people could possibly be. Surely they can go get their own boxes of food to throw a fancy party from the space station less than 10 seconds across the map! I found most of the quests were eye rolling at best and not really worth doing unless I absolutely needed the resource being offered as a reward.

Every once and awhile I would be unable to resume my economic conquest until I completed a story mission. Often I was so focused on the building aspect that I forgot about the story, only to groan at the reminder that I must now desist my min-maxing of my production line and go blow something up (#FirstWorldGamerProblems). Some native inhabitants of the moon decided to resort to terrorism to keep you from exploiting their precious hunk of rock for resources. They take out a bunch of arctic climate stabilizer thingys that would put the whole sensitive system in jeopardy. Unfortunately you cant just shrug and leave because the people who are giving you access to the arctic zones and its resources  absolutely need your help to stop the terrorists because they are utterly defenseless.

Honestly, I sympathized with the moon people, I was the bad guy in the whole altercation. I looked at their energy producing capabilities like an advanced nation looks at third world oil reserves. The whole story felt tacked on and wasn't at all engaging. Nor were the naval battles you had to complete to finish the story line missions. Yes the explosions were great but every map looked the same with only the number and strength of my ships and the enemy fleets providing any kind of change. Seriously, we can live on the moon, we have the technologywhere are the space battles?

Anno 2205 is an amazing city builder that takes a different spin on things and lets you create a vast empire. However, a better, more engaging storyline, possible one where you're not rooting for the bad guys and more interesting missions would have made for a much better experience. Its an example of trying to do too much and only succeeding at some of what you set out to do.
summary: Good, but some flaws are easier to overlook than others

reviewText:
In my opinion, FlatOut 2 is one of the best arcade style racing games ever. It may not have items, like Mario Kart or Crash Team Racing, but what it does have is Super speed, destruction Derby style racing. I'm personally a fan of this style of racing game, so there may be some bias, but the racing really is fun. You're encouraged to cause as much destruction as possible. While going at a high speed the control, realistically, becomes somewhat slippery, and makes racing challenging as you need the skill to know when to go fast and when to slow down a bit.
Plus, we have the stunts, where you launch your driver through the windshield in a variety of mini games, such as Baseball, Ring Of Fire, Ski Jump, and more. All of these are great time killers, and may be more fun than the actual racing.
Overall, FlatOut 2 is a great arcade style racing game, but if what I've described doesn't sound amazing to you, I'd recommend skipping this.

reviewText:
This is my third set of SteelSeries Siberia gaming headphones. My first pair (Frost Blue), now several years old, work as well today as on day one. However, their programmablity/customization is fairly limited. I can set the blue to bright or dim or "breathe" (which cycles from bright to dim and back). Here, these are HIGHLY customizable. You just need to go to the SteelSeries website and download the "SteelSeries Engine 3" ... this engine will even detect and update your old headphones.

The sound is highly customizable with the SteelSeries Engine. You can select from "game," "movie," or "music" mode. Likewise, you can use the graphic equalizer for further customization (as well as a variety of other pre-sets). Additionally, the microphone is retractable and repositionable as well as sensitive enough to pick-up on even a whisper.  The band was just a little tight, but they tend to loosen-up fairly fast (not too loose, of course).

And, I must admit, the reason I grabbed this pair was to completely customize the visually display pink hues. There are hundreds and hundreds of color options as well as effects (which includes cycling through colors ... so cute!)

For me, these were a great addition to my overall collection! Highly, highly recommended!

My son likes to wear headsets so he can play and talk without the rest of the family getting annoyed with the sound. His tv room is right next to our living room and it can get loud if he is playing and talking. These headphones are very nice! Way nicer than a cheapo headset. Not like a fancy one, but we don't really want to spend the money on a super high-end one that will get banged around for games. Maybe when he is older. My husband thought it was a little tight on his head (and his head is average to small in ball cap size) so we put it over the arm of our couch overnight to loosen it up. It did work, but we may need to do that again for a little longer time. I did take one star for that, especially for someone who has a larger than average head...not sure how far they will stretch. I suppose they had to pick a size that would fit kids and adults. It isn't painful, but bugs you after a while. My son does like Titanfall, so he likes the design very much.
If you have an Xbox One, this is not optional. Buy one of these, it will make your life better.

This is your typical Dynasty Warriors game with a Dragon Quest theme, but with a slight twist. For those who never played Dynasty warrior games; you are a overpowered character who fight a horde of  50+ very low level perfectionists (aside from mid-bosses, boss type characters) on screen who just huddle around in the group and let you pummel them to death unless they are 95% sure they can attack you. The difference between other Dynasty warriors games is that instead of capturing bases, majority of the objectives have you defending objects, wall or a person as hordes of enemies aims towards that particular target (sort of like a free range tower defense). You can capture monsters during battle and place them in certain location, but aside from that you have to keep an eye at what you're defending most of the time as the AI companions are not that reliable.

Story is pretty generic. Human and monsters live together in peace, but one day a wizard casts a spell to have the monsters fight against humans and it's up to our heroes to investigate the matter and save the day. But the banter between characters are enjoyable at times

Gameplay wise it's your standard attack, defend, dodge and special move mechanic you're used to. You can hop between 4 selectable party members (you can choose from 12 total) with varied attack abilities and attributes. When you do switch characters they tend of follow you like a dog on a leash, which can be advantageous against boss type enemies, but can be irritating when you want a party member to defend a specific location while you scout the map or attack at an different location. Summoned and captured monsters stay around the area where they are summon, so I do not understand why when you switch characters they tend to follow no matter what. There should be a command function to tell a party member to stay, defend, or follow you; or at least have an option for online or local co-op (which is not an option in the game). Boss fights are okay; it's a lot of pattern recognition as bosses can defeat you in 3-4 strong attacks (depending on your level). There's no lock on mechanic which makes boss battles a bit cumbersome especially when you're lower level and try to maneuver around their attacks.

Story missions are simple, but at times you have to grind for exp and/or money for weapon, armor and accessory upgrades to progress though the game as even though the enemies are easy to defeat they may have a lot of armor/HP taking time to take down. And seeing most of the game objectives are escort/defend an object type missions swarms of enemies can be troublesome especially if they have a lot of routes to reach their target. The game does provide you with a lot of side quests to alleviate this, but there's hardly any subtext to the story so it feels a lot like grinding to get strong enough to progress through the story. You can also keep playing the same level or and over again till you beat it as the exp and drops do carry over after each game over screen

Graphics are really good as it's very colorful and pleasant to look at. Instead of a realistic style, it has a more a cell shaded cartoon look and matches the Dragon Quest theme well.

Voice acting is really good, but doesn't match up with the characters on screen breaking the immersion. Most of characters have a deep English European voice in their late 40s, when the characters are depicted as if they're in their teens. Also there's a 6-7 year old in the game who you have save and bring back to her parents who has a voice of someone in her late 20s. Background music will sound very familiar to fans of the Dragon Quest series.

Platinum trophies is easy to get, but most trophies require a lot hours of grinding.

Overall if you like a free range tower defense/escort mission, dynasty warriors type game with a Dragon Quest theme this game may be for you. Just keep in mind there is grinding involved to progress the story.

The Nintendo 3DS XL combines next-generation portable gaming with 3D effects.  It brings a whole new world of gaming to a new generation and some new fun for us old gamers.

Features of the Nintendo 3DS XL:

Take 3D photos
Connect to friends and other players for multi-player gaming
Wireless access
90% larger screen than the previous 3DS
Two screens - bottom used with a stylus and the top displays 3D visuals
You can control the 3D effect by scaling it back or pumping it up to highest level - this is great because younger kids should not be using 3D. It also uses Face Tracking which automatically adjusts the 3D 'sweet spot' based on the location of the user's face.
C Stick for enhanced controls in certain games
Built-in amiibo compatibility & NFC support - just tap your amiibo on the touch screen.
Faster processing power than Nintendo 3DS which means faster loading of applications and downloading.
Folds up for easy transporting - it actually is not too much bigger than my smartphone!
Amazing graphics whether you use it 3D viewing or not
Great battery life - although it doesn't come with a charger so you will need to pick up a Nintendo 3DS/3DS XL/2DS AC Adapter (it uses the same adapter as other Nintendo DS's if you already own one.)
Nintendo store to shop apps
Stylus included and stores inside
YouTube - yes it has YouTube!

So I hadn't played a Nintendo 3DS before I received this one and I honestly wasn't sure what to expect. The whole 3D part without glasses just sounded strange to me but it actually works and yes you can turn it down to be a regular screen. It is pretty crazy to see and it did take me awhile to get used to but once I was used to it I was good to go. I usually keep it off (down) and the games are just fine. The graphics have definitely come a long way from the Classic GameBoy, they are pretty amazing.

As far as games go, you actually get a few right on the 3DS to play such as Face Raiders , Mii Maker, AR Games Nintendo and Mii Plaza. My 4 year old and I had a blast with the Face Raiders game. I was amazed at how fast she grasped to work the controls and she thought it was hysterical.

Since you only get the few games, you will definitely want to pick up a few more games for your child (or you to play.)  I was worried my kids wouldn't like the DS now they have played with a tablet but they were actually fighting over it. I think for them they like the games (characters) available and its not the same ole' same ole' tablet apps. The DS has traveled with us in the car, in restaurants and in blanket forts. We have a good selection of games now and my kids have no problem playing any of them.
Replaced broken one on my 3ds XL and it works like a charm.  Had my son do the work but would order again if necessary.

basically the exact same game engine as Zeus Master of Olympus, with a bunch of changes and some improvements and flavor stuff added.  Loads of variety of stuff and the ability to waste metric tons of goods to summon ancestral heroes to wander around your city.  music is also incredibly addictive.

despite all the good stuff tha tmakes the game fun and addictive to come back to, this game, more so than Zeus Master of Olympus, has a citizen AI that is so insanely frustrating that it might make you hate China.

Rather than having citizens go to markets to buy stuff like in Caesar III and Pharaoh, they expect the market cart to come to them, like in Zeus Master of Olympus.  This leads to tons of annoying circumstances where the market cart picks its route entirely at random, so you'll have houses a mile away fully upgraded with tons of food and supplies, and a house right next to the market a barren hut that has had no food delivered.  This is an all the time thing and you will ALWAYS have random barren huts with no food delivered scattered amidst bustling apartment metropoleis.

As well, they seem to be amazingly blazingly incompetent in terms of maintenance.  Whenever you build anything anywhere, it seems like you need at least 5-6 inspectors' towers (towers to prevent fires and building collapses) for every 8-10 buildings.  If you have any less, you'll have houses/buildings randomly collapse, and huge fires blaze out of control while inspectors from other towers further away just walk past like it's none of their business.  I've had entire cities collapse if I didn't have literally HALF the workforce employed JUST in inspectors towers.  It's as if every city has a pack of arsonists that only inspectors towers by the dozen can quell.

I can have a neighborhood lane with a road going down the middle and 4 houses on each side of the road, with 4 houses beside them and another road on each side.  There can be two inspectors towers, one on each vertical end of this lane.  And the middle two houses will ALWAYS collapse out of nowhere after a time.  The only option is to replace one of the middle houses with another inspectors tower, and the other house with gardens to prevent the Ugliness caused by the inspectors tower.  THIS IS NOT AN EXAGGERATION AT ALL DESPITE MY ALL CAPS SCREAMING.

The same goes for essentially everything else.  The most basic necessity is water from a well, and the scattershot AI is such that houses can be literally RIGHT NEXT TO A WELL but still stuck as common housing, the most basic house unit, because "The parched residents of this house need water if this house is to evolve" while they live RIGHT NEXT TO A WELL.  The most frustrating is Ancestral shrines, as you will have half your city inundated with ancestral shrines everywhere, and not only will the other half of the city lack access to ancestral shrines, but even the houses that DO have access to them will periodically devolve down to huts because they "lost access" to an ancestral shrine somehow.  No house type above barren Hut will ever stay stable for longer than a few months in-game before devolving due to a completely temporary/nonexistent loss of access to herbalist/acupuncturist/Daoist or Buddhist shrine/ancestral shrine/food/hemp/ceramics/music/drama/literally anything that lasts longer than an in-game week.

Nothing is stable with regards to supplies and needs being met, not due to any scarcity (you can have your city of 2000 flooded with enough food to feed 16000 and you'll still have 1/3rd of your city demanding food be delivered to them), but due to the completely half-dead AI.  As well, the harvesting of supplies seems to be entirely arbitrary as you can have a deluge of hemp or clay or wood, enough at any given time to satisfy everything in your city, and yet somehow have 0 of them in your warehouses, thus leaving you with a well-functioning city, whilst the Grand Temple that requires 3 stacks of wood to continue building goes for 10 or more in-game years with no progress because no one can spare 3 stacks of wood, even as you build 8 more logging sheds and start importing extra wood.

The other city's AI is even worse than in Zeus Master of Olympus; basically out of the starting cities in any scenario, 2/3rds of them will always refuse to open trade with you until you give them tons of free garbage first.  At random moments, certain cities will try to invade you, even while you are friendly and openly trading.  Other cities are permanently fixed to be invincible (such as the Nomad Camps/Xiognu Tribes) so that no matter how many hundreds of troops, complete with allied city assistance, and being led by Huang Di, the actual Yellow Emperor himself, you'll always lose, yet when they invade you, they bring some rinky dink horse archers and half a fort's worth of archers.

Invasions are pretty much a complete gamble, even when you have a full army of 12 stacks (the maximum).  And whenever you invade, there is approximately a 141% chance that the city you just conquered will instantly rebel against you the moment your troops leave it.  Sending your troops back has a 90% chance of them being defeated SOMEHOW, and the only way to get your vassal to give tribute again is to gift them tons of free garbage, EVEN MORE than is needed to bribe a city to trade with you.

Another game-breaking nonsensical thing that happens is completely random population drops precipitated by absolutely nothing.  Currently in one mission I have a booming metropolis of 10,000 people, we produce all our own food, hemp, ceramics, tea, import extra food, make a huge profit every year, treasury is nearly 200,000, plenty of room to expand, which I'm currently doing, and yet the city is FALLING APART because people keep leaving in huge numbers for no reason.  On average my city needs 100-200 workers, and every few months drops as high as needing 500 workers, leaving huge swaths of my city with barely functioning towers, production, harvesting, etc.  I build entire new neighborhoods, and people move in, and the population remains stagnant and I still need 200 workers.  Nothing stems this except completing the mission and leaving the cesspit of a city behind.
This is truly the game of the year in 2014 it is very fun not too short not too long. I would indefinitely play this game again.

The Mamba TE emerged as my second-favorite after the Steelseries Rival 300 in a field of a dozen mice. The Mamba TE scored points for aesthetics, size, shape, and weight, but it lost points for intrusive software, unnecessary lighting, slightly sticky surface, and laser rather than optical sensing. The Mamba TE has a sturdier and quieter scroll wheel than the DA Chroma, and it also has horizontal scrolling, although I seldom use this feature. Switches are well placed and responsive, but the front left-side switch made a hollow sound when touched that gave an impression of poor build quality.
summary: Better than DA Chroma; not Quite as Good as SS Rival 300

reviewText:
The worst keyboard I've ever used. It is not ergonomic at all. NOT at all. I bought this keyboard because I urgently needed to finish a major project (and my beloved Logitech G19 was ruined after I spilled a cup of coffee on it and something somewhere shorted out), so I went to a local store and over-payed some $50.00 to get this G910, figuring I could not go wrong with Logitech. I rarely regret my purchases anywhere because I don't buy much and if I buy, then it is a well-researched purchase of an item that I need.

I will try to make it short: Mechanically and otherwise technically, the keyboard is solid. The REAL problem is that the size of its keys is somewhere between a NORMAL 101 keyboard and that of a laptop. It is neither here nor there. And if you know anything about motor functions f your brain (translated to your limbs) you will easily see that this keyboard is very hard to get used to. I thought I could tough it out and adjust...

Now, several months later, I still hit keys I don't intend to hit and it REALLY gets in the way of my work (which involves A LOT OF TYPING).

To be fair, I don't use smartphones (and the port for those phones seems to be the selling point for the G910).

GET A G19 while they are still available. A G19 has a nice screen for all your game and multimedia data and more and you will not mistype on G19 because its key layout is perfect.
summary: The worst keyboard I've ever used

Bought for Elite Dangerous at $50, this also works much better than my very old Microsoft Sidewinder joystick on X3. Very good modern joystick for the price-point, to get a Hall Effect joystick you would need to triple the price. Just keep it covered up in a tote or put plastic shopping bags over it when not using it if you live in a dusty environment like I do, dust made my older joystick feel gritty once it got in. Also the ability to separate the base is handy, for easier storage and to keep it compact I keep the base together. There are two screws that hold it together you need to tighten.

Nice thing is using the 3 side buttons on the throttle as modifiers. Hold it down and you have a new set of commands, let off you got original controls on the stick. On Elite you need like arrow keys to press up, down, left and right to assign power to engines, weapons, or systems. I hold down one of the side buttons on throttle and just use the POV hat. Quick and intuitive, I wouldn't think of doing that until I read another review on this stick before buying it.

Overall happy with purchase, very good joystick at moderate price. It's more than the $30-$35 joysticks, sure, but it's barely a step up in price compared to anything above it which quickly shoots up beyond $100. It's accurate and smooth, it feels really good and I have a few space games I play so I plan on using it plenty. I am also certain this will work for plane games very well.
summary: Very good stick for price, works great for Elite Dangerous

This is a great functional design and working product.  I was using traditional AA Batteries when I first got my X Box One, however they would last no more than 8 hours of use on (less with games that use a lot of rumbling) so I was going through a pair of AA batteries usually every 2-3 days.  Its also clear that the controllers have a high minimum threshold for the battery as batteries I have taken from these have been inserted in other devices (like radios and flashlights) and still have a significant charge in them.  A rechargeable solution such as this Nyko Charge Block was basically a must.

Two problems with it however:  First off the white variant (as I have a White X Box and controller) costs more.  This only bothers me mildly, as I bought the black one anyway since I don't care about the look...but it feels wrong to me.  Secondly the indicator for charging.  These blocks blink on and off when charging, which is ridiculously bright as well and emits from both sides of the block.  I cannot even imagine what it must be like to have 4 of these bricked together all blinking...the light is extremely bright.  A better solution would have been to have a two color system to indicate charging and charged...such as red for charging and green for completely charged.
summary: A much needed solution, with a minor drawback.

reviewText:
I've never been especially keen on the Tolkienesque "fantasy" realms w/ elves, dwarves, wizards, dragons, & such...  but this game has nudged me more in that direction than I ever expected to go.  So much fun to be had, and I've barely scratched the surface (I've played the FALLOUT series, so I comprehend the sort of scale that Bethesda brings to their games, and I'm nowhere near an eighth of the way through -- even after hours and hours of play).

Takes a bit of patience to learn the interface ropes when it comes to inventory, ranking system, and such (comparably complicated to FALLOUT's clunky system), but if you have some patience with that stuff you'll get to the great joy of this game.  I'd actually recommend checking YouTube for tutorials about using the interface, just to smooth it along.

Incidentally, the game is MUCH more fun to play than to spectate, by the way.

You do need to cut the graphics a bit of slack compared to current-gen games, but there is plenty of beauty and visual grandeur to be found.
summary: HEMMED & HAWED ABOUT THIS?  TIME TO GET IT!

reviewText:
The Good : It works fine with 1 or 2 controllers, including wireless ones (where the wireless dongle goes in the plug). The controller input for both wires and wireless is crisp and responsive. Great for PS2 emulators.

The Bad : Doesn't register as an Xinput device (i.e. XBox 360 compatible). That isn't an issue for most emulators, but is for many PC games, where it isn't recognized at all. There is a program called "Xbox 360ce" which can make it act like a 360 gamepad for most games.

Note : This wouldn't work at all  without a driver for me, and I had no way to use the included CD. I found it online. Do a search on "epforum driver for blue triangular playstation" and read the forum thread. It has the drivers you need, including one to help with vibration support.
summary: Works fine but read for specific issue fixes...

Don't be scared off by the cartoon-ish look - I'm a hardcore COD / Battlefield junkie and held off on this for a long time, but Overwatch lives up to the hype and is a fantastic FPS. The gameplay is exceptionally smooth, and as another reviewer put it, Overwatch is easy to learn and hard to master. I really like that each character has his or her own set of skills and moves. That adds so much depth to the gameplay.

The only downside is that the gameplay can get a little repetitive. There are a number of good maps, a lot of characters (and they're adding more, for free, regularly), but there are only a few game modes and no campaign, which cane make the game start to feel tired. All things considered, though, I'm very happy I picked up Overwatch and highly recommend it.

Mass Effect Andromeda was a fun game and a solid addition to the ME franchise. Unfortunately, it could have been even better but left me wanting a bit. As always I split my reviews into 3 categories: Story, Gameplay, Characters with story getting more weight

Story: 7/10
I really enjoyed the majority of the story. The main storyline was interesting and engaging. You play as Ryder, a human in the andromeda galaxy that leads the initiative in finding a home (or homes) for the travelers that came from the Milky Way. Each world you settle had it's own interesting story line but some things just left you scratching your head. Why put a giant monster in one world that you can't engage? That's just lazy and sloppy writing right there. Main plot points were never answered (benefactor?? don't really know much about the Jordaan, who are the Kett?). Some of the story lines behind some of your crew were just...lacking..more on that later. Overall the story was good, but there were so many details that weren't finished or fleshed out enough.

Gameplay: 9/10
Here is where the game shines. They've refined the ME gameplay even further and made some drastic improvements. The worlds are amazing, beautiful, and the gameplay keeps you coming back and never gets boring or annoying. The Nomad is really fun. Graphics are great. Battle mechanics are good. Why it doesn't get a perfect score though is there are enough things here that detract from the overall experience. More than a few bugs to deal with. And I really thought the level up system could have been improved. Depending on how much you play you can easily max out your character several hours before the story line concludes.

Characters 6.5/10
Usually in bioware games (at least years ago) the characters and character developments were really a shining point in their games. The first 3 Mass Effect games and Dragon Age 1 really had some great characters. But since then...not so much. The characters were fine. Jaal was interesting I thought. Suvi and Kallo were nice side characters. Drak was fun. But the rest just didn't have a whole lot to offer. None were annoying so that was nice. But I could have used quite a bit more depth. And where were the quarians? Yeah i know their Ark hadn't shown up yet but that's sloppy. They were a favorite of mine. The crew member's sidequests were almost the exact same just in a different location. Very lazy. Peebee's I felt was the best but probably still doesn't hold a candle to the earlier ME's characters and their sidequests.

Overall 7.5/10 Definitely worth playing and I don't think it's as bad as many make it out to be. But at the same time I see where the haters are coming from. If you like the ME series then it's definitely worth a shot but if you've never played one before, it might be better to start with one of the others.
summary: A solid addition to the franchise but fell short of it's potential

Choosing between buying Revelations from Nintendo shop vs Amazon, I recommend buying the code from Amazon. I was able to get the code fairly easily after purchase, type the code, download (~10 mins), and start playing the Revelation path. Personally, I find the Nintendo eShop on 3DS clunky or rather the idea of having to type in credit card info with my stylus tedious. It's also great if you have a credit from Amazon from choosing No-Rush shipping.

That said, the DLC itself...

If you've enjoyed Birthright and/or Conquest route (I recommend finishing both though), you'll enjoy Revelation. What's great about Revelation is, as other reviews have said, you are getting gameplay of both Birthright + Conquest, mixed in one; items, weapons, and classes from Birthright and Conquest will be readily available to you as you progress through chapters. Battles are not that challenging as Conquest, but more than Birthright. Maps have more variety than Birthright and Conquest as well although sometimes not for the challenge, but more for sake of variety than FE games in the past. Leveling up, having enough gold, and economy should be no problem in Revelation as it all just accumulates naturally and easily than I had trouble in Conquest. Really, Revelation should be easy for returning FE players. For new players to FE series, I recommend Birthright as that's more straightforward.

When I speak of variety of maps, I felt some of the objectives did feel rather gimmicky. At first, I was like "Woah! Never seen that in a FE game before!" but it didn't really add to the challenge most of the chapters as long as you had your OP units with you. Literally, in one chapter, the objective is the break down snow walls to get to boss to seize the map. That's the most gimmicky map I've ever experienced in a FE game. Some were on that line of gimmicky, but had that element of surprise that I enjoyed playing it. But the game isn't that hard compared to Conquest, the hardest of the three.

Man, just the art style! I have to say I think Fates has one of the eye popping visuals than other FE games! The style is very saturated; if it's going for the fantasy Europe for Nohr, it goes really there. If it's going for fantasy Feudal Japan, it goes really there, too. Not all characters caught my eye immediately, but most characters in Fates had my attention from just a look alone (I'm looking at you, Lobster Lord Ryoma XP). Fates' style makes other FE worlds more conservative in its fantasy.

Voice acting is fantastic. Like Awakening, it's only fully voiced in short cutscenes and has snippets of voice during dialogue. That's better than classic FE when voice acting was only in very limited cutscenes and all text, or just texts, no voice. Even with the download code, it's only English dubbed voice acting, no option for Japanese unlike in Awakening. There's no option for you to switch, which was a bit disappointing when I first started to play Fates, but didn't mind as the acting was great. Actually, I prefer the English dubbed Azura's song if I'm being honest. (Actress Rena Strober)

Music is just fantastic. If you upgrade the record hall to level 3, you can listen to the entire soundtrack, Nohr, Hoshido, and Revelation versions, in an infinite loop. It's great!

The one problem I have with Fates, including Revelation, is the story. Revelation, a DLC that's the 3rd, final, and most canon path of the three, does not answer all the questions about Fates FE universe. It clears away some details, but muddies a lot of things in my opinion. And stories of Fates follow the definition of melodrama. It is so melodramatic that it's unintentionally comedic.  More I think about Corrin/Kamui's backstory, it gives me a headache. I had a hard time buying into this universe when I first started playing Birthright and I had a hard time buying into what was being played out in front of me in Revelation. So, the story is not the greatest compared to other FE games, but I still enjoyed ride because I fell in love with both Hoshido and Conquest characters.

Playing Revelation is really worth it if you've liked Birthright and/or Conquest. It is definitely not the first game out of the three to start Fates as the plot is just ... a mess. I don't really recommend Fates as the first Fire Emblem game for new players. The devs clearly produced the full game in three versions, not just one, for the full experience. But for FE fans, Fates is worth trying out and if you've liked Birthright and Conquest then Revelation is worth trying out, too.
summary: Conquest + Birthright characters in one?! Ship away, shippers! Ship away!!!

reviewText:
To cover each of those points:

"The Dungeon Movement sucks." The camera is controlled by the L and R buttons, as well as the Dpad. Attacking an enemy while moving around has a delay that feels too long. There's three camera angles of close, far away, and awkwardly-over-the-shoulder. The enemies also appear in the over-world as a weird mass of blue blocks that clashes with just about every setting in the game.

"Combat is decent." That's a lie, this is SMT where the combat is eternally broken in your favor. Once you get demons the game becomes exponentially easier. Spoiler: that's the first five minutes of the game. Standard SMT systems for the most part, standard seven elements if you count physical, plus talking to the enemy, as is standard. Party size is lower than I'm used to for the 3DS SMT games, with only a party size of 4 active combatants at a time, as opposed to the 6 we had in Soul Hackers, or the ridiculous 12 party members at a time in the Devil Survivor games, but that actually is a good thing as it makes things feel a bit more focused, and gives a little less room to cover everything with one party, leading to strategy gaining some more importance.

"Story is standard SMT for the most part" Multiple ending paths that involve aligning with angels, demons, or just not. Setting is interesting and seems to be related to other games, but like always nothing is concrete.

"MC's hair reminds me of Mara." That wasn't a joke. Just look at it. It looks like Mara rolled around in brown paint and was slapped down on the MC's head. Poor guy, must have been traumatic.

If my opinions change on those first three points I'll come back to edit this, but that last point will likely never change.
summary: The dungeon movement sucks. Combat is decent. Story is standard SMT for the most part. The MC's hair reminds me of Mara.

I've always thought Rockstar was one-step from making GTA into a perfect game but they always had to get too cute with GTA. Not with RDR, even the Undead DLC is a ton of fun. What's more fun than jumping on an automatic mounted chain-machine gun and mowing down hordes of zombies? I think a big part of the fun with RDR is the main character John. Unlike GTA, especially V, you don't feel a polarizing feeling towards John. The story and his backstory draws you in. You could be just like him .. you know, if you lived in the old west. You're going to get attached to the character, his family and you'll even enjoy the parts that normally would feel repetitive. I will say Skyrim and Fallout are also good at doing this. All video games, in my 30+ years of gaming, become boring. They drag. It's going to happen but if the developer can make you feel like you're accomplishing something for your character, you don't notice it as much. RDR does that. I'll give an example with GTA. I really like the GTA games but I eventually get tired of "playing by the rules". Like when GTA San Andreas had you destroying the comic book guy's remote helicopter toys and you had to use a remote helicopter toy to do it. In RDR, there's no real restrictions, if you can kill those legendary animals with a knife, have at it. Want to throw dynamite at them? You can. Or you find them from a distance, get your Carcano Rifle out and try to snipe the elusive beast.

My teenage son said it best, "Everything in it feels epic". I think that's what makes this game so great. From the side challenges like trying to skin X amount of a certain wild animal to trying to poke a knife between your fingers in a QTE game to my personal favorite moment of the game. You've just realized you have a bunch of animal skins to sell but haven't saved. Then you hear it. The sound of a cougar attacking. Those will kill you before you can get your gun pulled, kill your horse too. It's such a fun, intense moment in a usually mid-paced game. The characters throughout the game are either crazy or hilarious or both. Just wait until you have to run behind the bank and hunt the critters. It's a relatively violent, harsh game that doesn't use gore or shock to make it violent. It's almost like an old west simulator. I've had Red Dead Redemption 2 preordered since last summer. Even worse, I decided to switch from the XB1 to PS4, so now I'm going to have to cancel my XB1 preorder and preorder it on PS4. I would rate it as one of the greatest games ever made. The game that comes the closest in old west style is Gun. Nowhere near as challenging as RDR but similar gameplay and setting. By the way, it works great on BC. I've played it all the way through twice on 360, once on XB1 BC and wouldn't be surprised if I try it on PS Now.

The mouse itself is pretty solid and does everything you'd expect and feels good in the hand, however I have never had a mouse break on me this quickly. It broke almost exactly one year after the fact. The scroll wheel nearly completely stopped working. They do have a 2 year warranty at least. The main reason I'm giving it 3 stars is every mouse I have ever owned has not malfunctioned this quickly.
summary: The mouse itself is pretty solid and does everything you'd expect and feels good ...

reviewText:
The sound is fantastic, beyond that they are just horribly designed in every possible way.

Pros:

Battery life is good.
Audio quality is good.

Cons:

Getting this thing to charch properly in the base is infuriating.  It never makes proper contact, and if it does, it doesn't maintain contact for long and shifts ever so slightly that it stops charging.  Just buy a USB cable and charge it through the charger port on the side.

Microphone is pretty poor quality.  I have significantly cheaper headsets that the microphone audio quality is substantially superior.  If they would have just abandoned the money they threw away on terrible R&D for the charge base, they could have invested in a microphone that didn't sound awful.

The signal only works on a very narrow channel and band that can't be modified, causing literally any wireless router or access point released in the last 15 years to interfere with it.  If you live in an apartment, college dorm, anywhere you can see multiple wifi networks, don't even bother buying these.

Signal strength is atrocious.  You can't go through a single room without it starting to crackle and break up, then the connection just dies (we're talking 15 feet, through one wall with no plumbing or major electrical).
summary: Even if it were half the price, there are better headsets out there.

reviewText:
Graphics- Really good. Lighting is awesome and the game play is smooth. It has a nice blur effect that helps you feel the speed.

Audio- Solid. Some items sound a bit forward but you can adjust it and find a good balance.

Game Play- Overall it is really good. The tracks are nicely done and the cars feel fleshed out. The AI is okay but at times can be bothersome.

Content- I wish there was 1000+ cars, it has under 200 at this time. It needs all of the tracks from all of the previous games. It is missing some must haves like Infineon, Laguna Seca, Fuji Speedway, Cape Ring, Apricot Hill, Spa, Circut De La Sarth, and others. In 16 days I have completed 97% of the content available. Past GT's have taken me a few months to complete. They are adding free content updates but at this slow rate it will take years to get all the great cars and tracks back. One make races are missing. Only two Porsche races. Almost no focus on building your car into a race machine. I think many players want to take a car and mod it as they progress. Gut it for weight, add a roll cage, power it up, swap the transmission, add an aero kit and so on.

Online- Pretty bad. The penalties also hurt the immersion. I do not like the fact that my game data  and save files are on their servers. If the server is offline you can not access your content.

Couldnt finish the game because it kept crashing at the same spot. War in the North does have a bad history with ATI / Radeon, but still, this is what I tried.
- Verified the file cache
- Tried various compatibility modes
- Tried installing the Large Address Aware patch
- Started a new game (twice) (in case the save was corrupted)
Still resulted in repeated crashing in Chapter 2.

Thoughts on the game before I reached the unplayable moment (I only played as a single-player):

Total size on my hard-drive: 7.4 GB

+ Ran well until the crashing started. Maxed 1440p produced a solid 60fps on my RX 470.
+ Decent story from what I could tell. Begin in Bree at the Prancing Pony, where we briefly meet Aragorn (who has heard tell of some hobbits coming south carrying a precious burden). He lays out our task: there are agents of Sauron in the North who are also doing the evil lords wishes, and it is our duty to find them, expose them, distract them, and generally cause as much trouble as possible. The linear levels help keep the narrative focused.
+ Decent animations. Walk, run, combat, faces  all are of a very high level for a game from 2011. Good finishing moves, too.

+/- Very linear levels.
+/- Limited use of destructible environments. Nice when they appear, but its not often.
+/- Graphics remind me of Dragon Age IIs. Simplistic but quite nice at times.
+/- Stupidly simple combat. Played single-player, the game seems to be begging for a Dragon Age-style tactics menu to create a pattern of behavior for the others in the squad. But there simply isn't enough depth in the combat to make use of such a system if it were there. Mash left-click, and when a small yellow triangle appears over the head of an enemy, right-click to get a critical hit (you can right-click at any time for a heavy attack, but it takes so long youll do the same damage just by mashing left-click). There are skill trees that seem to make some difference on combat, but I never got far enough for them to make much difference. Dodging is also sometimes required. Bosses are damage sponges.
+/- The three characters all have unique combat styles. Though the half-elven mage is an obvious support class who mostly uses her staff to hit people with, not launch spells. Magic is generally disappointing. The Ranger archer fights best from a distance (obviously) who isn't too quick to switch to the sword he carries. The dwarf soldier is the heavy hitter, and the one with the worst AI, so he is the best choice for a single-player experience.
+/- A nice amount of loot. Travel back to hubs often to sell stuff.
+/- Simplistic crafting to make potions and slottable gems. No tutorial for this. I stumbled across it because I noticed something that looked like a gem slot on a weapon.

- Character customization is awful. Choose from presets. Thats it.
- Playing single-player, you can only switch between characters at the end of a level. Or by quitting to the menu, selecting a different character, then loading the game.

Not recommended because it kept crashing on me, but before I reached that point War in the North seemed like a well-executed, slightly boring hack n slash whose primary appeal is to Lord of the Rings fans. Crashing aside, theres nothing obviously incompetent about it, but nothing stands out either. A curiosity purchase, nothing more.
summary: Technical problems mar an average experience

This is truly an awful game. I do not think those people who rate it higher than 2 stars actually know a lot about what engaging game mechanics and/or the notion of value for money mean. Most of the time you are just watching on the sidelines with very long cut scenes and with very little engagement options in between. Then, you make a simple choice, and the game just rolls on (usually, without your input again for a long time). At best, this is merely a cinematic experience. At worst, with such a slow-moving pace, locked-in camera angles and an ever-shifting angle of view, it is a frustrating effort to be authentic. All the characters are very annoying, hard to believe, and act like 5-year olds. The story is boring, artificially suspenseful, and with many plot holes, difficult to tolerate. I have played my share of games over some 40+ years, and boy, oh boy, was this one of the worst, and also one of the shortest if you did not actually bother to go for the collectibles. My suggestion: Go buy something else for the same price bracket. There are many better games out there, even with similar themes which you may find way more entertaining.
summary: Boring, unengaging, slow, with limited area for improvisation. Poor value for money. Go for something else.

reviewText:
Tis a pretty nice keyboard and has a good amount of feedback when I press the keys, which I like. My keyboard that I'm replacing felt too worn in and the keys would press too easily. There isn't much noise when the keys are pressed. The lights on it aren't deafening like a lot of other gaming keyboards, which is a plus.

The mouse is solid, even though I'm using another mouse already for my gaming macros.

So if you want a simple no frills gaming keyboard and mouse, here's your set.

The only con I can find, which is a big one, is the placement of the Forward Slash key, which in turn moves the Enter key too far to the right. I'm really only going to be using this keyboard for gaming, so it's not that big of deal, but the few occasional times I'll be using this keyboard to type, aka right now, I don't like that placement.
summary: Works well, lights unoffensive, and quiet compared to most gaming keyboards, with one flaw- the enter key

reviewText: These work very well on my Rock Candy, wired controller, which did not have rubberized stick tops.  They fit snuggly on top, hold well, do not slip around, and give me considerably better control.  Comes four in a pack.  It even want well with the red color of the controller.
